could potentially discriminate $f(R)$ models from the general relativity at
$\sim5\sigma$ level using the shape of the galaxy pairwise kSZ dipole $P_{{\rm
kSZ},\ell=1}$ alone, when $f_{R0}=10^{-4}$.

Phenomenology similar to the nonreciprocal charge transport violating
Onsagers reciprocity relations can develop in directionally inhomogeneous
conducting films with nonuniform Hall coefficient along the current trajectory.
The effect is demonstrated in ferromagnetic CoPd films and analyzed in
comparison with the unidirectional magnetoresistance phenomena. We suggest to
use an engineered inhomogeneity for spintronics applications and present the
concept of mixed symmetry Hall devices in which transverse to current Hall
signal is measured in a longitudinal contacts arrangement. Magnetization
reversal and memory detection is demonstrated in the three terminal and the
partitioned normal metal-ferromagnet (NM - FM) device designs. Multi-bit memory
is realized in the partitioned FM-NM-FM structure. The relative amplitude of
the antisymmetric signal in the engineered ferromagnetic devices is few percent
which is 10 to 1000 times higher than in their unidirectional magnetoresistance
analogues.

This article aims at establishing new benchmark scenarios for Galactic
cosmic-ray propagation in the GV-TV rigidity range, based on fits to the AMS-02
B/C data with the USINE v3.5 propagation code. We employ a new fitting
procedure, cautiously taking into account data systematic error correlations in
different rigidity bins and considering Solar modulation potential and leading
nuclear cross-section as nuisance parameters. We delineate specific low,
intermediate, and high-rigidity ranges that can be related to both features in
the data and peculiar microphysics mechanisms resulting in spectral breaks. We
single out a scenario which yields excellent fits to the data and includes all
the presumably relevant complexity, the BIG model. This model has two limiting
regimes: (i) the SLIM model, a minimal diffusion-only setup, and (ii) the
QUAINT model, a convection-reacceleration model where transport is tuned by
non-relativistic effects. All models lead to robust predictions in the
high-energy regime ($\gtrsim10$GV), i.e. independent of the propagation
scenario: at $1\sigma$, the diffusion slope $\delta$ is $[0.43-0.53]$, whereas
$K_{10}$, the diffusion coefficient at 10GV, is $[0.26-0.36]$kpc$^2$Myr$^{-1}$;
we confirm the robustness of the high-energy break, with a typical value
$\Delta_h\sim 0.2$. We also find a hint for a similar (reversed) feature at low
rigidity around the B/C peak ($\sim 4$GV) which might be related to some
effective damping scale in the magnetic turbulence.

For the next step in human to machine interaction, Artificial Intelligence
(AI) should interact predominantly using natural language because, if it
worked, it would be the fastest way to communicate. Facebook's toy tasks (bAbI)
provide a useful benchmark to compare implementations for conversational AI.
While the published experiments so far have been based on exploiting the
distributional hypothesis with machine learning, our model exploits natural
language understanding (NLU) with the decomposition of language based on Role
and Reference Grammar (RRG) and the brain-based Patom theory. Our combinatorial
system for conversational AI based on linguistics has many advantages: passing
bAbI task tests without parsing or statistics while increasing scalability. Our
model validates both the training and test data to find 'garbage' input and
output (GIGO). It is not rules-based, nor does it use parts of speech, but
instead relies on meaning. While Deep Learning is difficult to debug and fix,
every step in our model can be understood and changed like any non-statistical
computer program. Deep Learning's lack of explicable reasoning has raised
opposition to AI, partly due to fear of the unknown. To support the goals of
AI, we propose extended tasks to use human-level statements with tense, aspect
and voice, and embedded clauses with junctures: and answers to be natural
language generation (NLG) instead of keywords. While machine learning permits
invalid training data to produce incorrect test responses, our system cannot
because the context tracking would need to be intentionally broken. We believe
no existing learning systems can currently solve these extended natural
language tests. There appears to be a knowledge gap between NLP researchers and
linguists, but ongoing competitive results such as these promise to narrow that
gap.

Supersymmetry searches at the LHC are both highly varied and highly
constraining, but the vast majority are focused on cases where the final-stage
visible decays are prompt. Scenarios featuring superparticles with
detector-scale lifetimes have therefore remained a tantalizing possibility for
sub-TeV SUSY, since explicit limits are relatively sparse. Nonetheless, the
extremely low backgrounds of the few existing searches for collider-stable and
displaced new particles facilitates recastings into powerful long-lived
superparticle searches, even for models for which those searches are highly
non-optimized. In this paper, we assess the status of such models in the
context of baryonic R-parity violation, gauge mediation, and mini-split SUSY.
We explore a number of common simplified spectra where hadronic decays can be
important, employing recasts of LHC searches that utilize different detector
systems and final-state objects. The LSP/NLSP possibilities considered here
include generic colored superparticles such as the gluino and light-flavor
squarks, as well as the lighter stop and the quasi-degenerate Higgsino
multiplet motivated by naturalness. We find that complementary coverage over
large swaths of mass and lifetime is achievable by superimposing limits,
particularly from CMS's tracker-based displaced dijet search and heavy stable
charged particle searches. Adding in prompt searches, we find many cases where
a range of sparticle masses is now excluded from zero lifetime to infinite
lifetime with no gaps. In other cases, the displaced searches furnish the only
extant limits at any lifetime.

In this paper we describe the main features of the software package named
FITSH, intended to provide a standalone environment for analysis of data
acquired by imaging astronomical detectors. The package provides utilities both
for the full pipeline of subsequent related data processing steps (incl. image
calibration, astrometry, source identification, photometry, differential
analysis, low-level arithmetic operations, multiple image combinations, spatial
transformations and interpolations, etc.) and for aiding the interpretation of
the (mainly photometric and/or astrometric) results. The package also features
a consistent implementation of photometry based on image subtraction, point
spread function fitting and aperture photometry and provides easy-to-use
interfaces for comparisons and for picking the most suitable method for a
particular problem. This set of utilities found in the package are built on the
top of the commonly used UNIX/POSIX shells (hence the name of the package),
therefore both frequently used and well-documented tools for such environments
can be exploited and managing massive amount of data is rather convenient.

We study a close relationship between the topological anti-de Sitter
(TAdS)-black holes and topological de Sitter (TdS) spaces including the
Schwarzschild-de Sitter (SdS) black hole in five-dimensions. We show that all
thermal properties of the TdS spaces can be found from those of the TAdS black
holes by replacing $k$ by $-k$. Also we find that all thermal information for
the cosmological horizon of the SdS black hole is obtained from either the
hyperbolic-AdS black hole or the Schwarzschild-TdS space by substituting $m$
with $-m$. For this purpose we calculate thermal quantities of bulk,
(Euclidean) conformal field theory (ECFT) and moving domain wall by using the
A(dS)/(E)CFT correspondences. Further we compute logarithmic corrections to the
Bekenstein-Hawking entropy, Cardy-Verlinde formula and Friedmann equation due
to thermal fluctuations. It implies that the cosmological horizon of the TdS
spaces is nothing but the event horizon of the TAdS black holes and the dS/ECFT
correspondence is valid for the TdS spaces in conjunction with the AdS/CFT
correspondence for the TAdS black holes.

Graph neural networks (GNNs) have witnessed an unprecedented proliferation in
tackling several problems in computer vision, computer-aided diagnosis, and
related fields. While prior studies have focused on boosting the model
accuracy, quantifying the reproducibility of the most discriminative features
identified by GNNs is still an intact problem that yields concerns about their
reliability in clinical applications in particular. Specifically, the
reproducibility of biological markers across clinical datasets and distribution
shifts across classes (e.g., healthy and disordered brains) is of paramount
importance in revealing the underpinning mechanisms of diseases as well as
propelling the development of personalized treatment. Motivated by these
issues, we propose, for the first time, reproducibility-based GNN selection
(RG-Select), a framework for GNN reproducibility assessment via the
quantification of the most discriminative features (i.e., biomarkers) shared
between different models. To ascertain the soundness of our framework, the
reproducibility assessment embraces variations of different factors such as
training strategies and data perturbations. Despite these challenges, our
framework successfully yielded replicable conclusions across different training
strategies and various clinical datasets. Our findings could thus pave the way
for the development of biomarker trustworthiness and reliability assessment
methods for computer-aided diagnosis and prognosis tasks. RG-Select code is
available on GitHub at https://github.com/basiralab/RG-Select.

A recent study reported development of Muscorian, a generic text processing
tool for extracting protein-protein interactions from text that achieved
comparable performance to biomedical-specific text processing tools. This
result was unexpected since potential errors from a series of text analysis
processes is likely to adversely affect the outcome of the entire process. Most
biomedical entity relationship extraction tools have used biomedical-specific
parts-of-speech (POS) tagger as errors in POS tagging and are likely to affect
subsequent semantic analysis of the text, such as shallow parsing. This study
aims to evaluate the parts-of-speech (POS) tagging accuracy and attempts to
explore whether a comparable performance is obtained when a generic POS tagger,
MontyTagger, was used in place of MedPost, a tagger trained in biomedical text.
Our results demonstrated that MontyTagger, Muscorian's POS tagger, has a POS
tagging accuracy of 83.1% when tested on biomedical text. Replacing MontyTagger
with MedPost did not result in a significant improvement in entity relationship
extraction from text; precision of 55.6% from MontyTagger versus 56.8% from
MedPost on directional relationships and 86.1% from MontyTagger compared to
81.8% from MedPost on nondirectional relationships. This is unexpected as the
potential for poor POS tagging by MontyTagger is likely to affect the outcome
of the information extraction. An analysis of POS tagging errors demonstrated
that 78.5% of tagging errors are being compensated by shallow parsing. Thus,
despite 83.1% tagging accuracy, MontyTagger has a functional tagging accuracy
of 94.6%.

The single stable isotope of beryllium is a pure product of cosmic-ray
spallation in the ISM. Assuming that the cosmic-rays are globally transported
across the Galaxy, the beryllium production should be a widespread process and
its abundance should be roughly homogeneous in the early-Galaxy at a given
time. Thus, it could be useful as a tracer of time. In an investigation of the
use of Be as a cosmochronometer and of its evolution in the Galaxy, we found
evidence that in a log(Be/H) vs. [alpha/Fe] diagram the halo stars separate
into two components. One is consistent with predictions of evolutionary models
while the other is chemically indistinguishable from the thick-disk stars. This
is interpreted as a difference in the star formation history of the two
components and suggests that the local halo is not a single uniform population
where a clear age-metallicity relation can be defined. We also found evidence
that the star formation rate was lower in the outer regions of the thick disk,
pointing towards an inside-out formation.

In this paper, we study the effect of population imbalance and its interplay
with pairing strength and lattice effect in atomic Fermi gases in a
one-dimensional optical lattice. We compute various phase diagrams as the
system undergoes BCS-BEC crossover, using the same pairing fluctuation theory
as in Part I. We find widespread pseudogap phenomena beyond the BCS regime and
intermediate temperature superfluid states for relatively low population
imbalances. The Fermi surface topology plays an important role in the behavior
of $T_\text{c}$. For large $d$ and/or small $t$, which yield an open Fermi
surface, superfluidity can be readily destroyed by a small amount of population
imbalance $p$. The superfluid phase, especially in the BEC regime, can exist
only for a highly restricted volume of the parameter space. Due to the
continuum-lattice mixing, population imbalance gives rise to a new mechanism
for pair hopping, as assisted by excessive majority fermions, which may lead to
significant enhancement of $T_\text{c}$ on the BEC side of the Feshbach
resonance, and also render $T_\text{c}$ approaching a constant asymptote in the
BEC limit, when it exists. Furthermore, we find that not all minority fermions
will be paired up in BEC limit, unlike the 3D continuum case. These predictions
can be tested in future experiments.

Aims: The binary pulsar PSR J1811-1736 has been identified, since its
discovery, as a member of a double neutron star system. Observations of such
binary pulsars allow the measurement of general relativistic effects, which in
turn lead to information about the orbiting objects and, in a few cases, to
tests of theories of gravity. Methods: Regular timing observations have been
carried out with three of the largest European radio telescopes involved in
pulsar research. The prospects of continued observations were studied with
simulated timing data. Pulse scattering times were measured using dedicated
observations at 1.4 GHz and at 3.1 GHz, and the corresponding spectral index
has also been determined. The possibility of detecting the yet unseen companion
as a radio pulsar was also investigated. A study of the natal kick received by
the younger neutron star at birth was performed. Results: We present an up to
date and improved timing solution for the binary pulsar PSR J1811-1736. One
post-Keplerian parameter, the relativistic periastron advance, is measured and
leads to the determination of the total mass of this binary system. The pulse
profile at 1.4 GHz is heavily broadened by interstellar scattering, limiting
the timing precision achievable at this frequency and the measurability of
other post-keplerian parameters. Interstellar scattering is unlikely to be the
reason for the continued failure to detect radio pulsations from the companion
of PSR J1811-1736. The probability distribution that we derive for the
amplitude of the kick imparted on the companion neutron star at its birth
indicates that the kick has been of low amplitude.

We investigate Cu-doped Ba(Fe$_{1-x}$Cu$_x$)$_2$As$_2$ with transport,
magnetic susceptibility, and elastic neutron scattering measurements. In the
heavily Cu-doped regime where long-range stripe-type antiferromagnetic order in
BaFe$_2$As$_2$ is suppressed, Ba(Fe$_{1-x}$Cu$_x$)$_2$As$_2$ (0.145 $\leq x
\leq$ 0.553) samples exhibit spin-glass-like behavior in magnetic
susceptibility and insulating-like temperature dependence in electrical
transport. Using elastic neutron scattering, we find stripe-type short-range
magnetic order in the spin-glass region identified by susceptibility
measurements. The persistence of short-range magnetic order over a large doping
range in Ba(Fe$_{1-x}$Cu$_x$)$_2$As$_2$ likely arises from local arrangements
of Fe and Cu that favor magnetic order, with Cu acting as vacancies relieving
magnetic frustration and degeneracy. These results indicate locally broken
four-fold rotational symmetry, suggesting that stripe-type magnetism is
ubiquitous in iron pnictides.

Light field cameras can capture both spatial and angular information of light
rays, enabling 3D reconstruction by a single exposure. The geometry of 3D
reconstruction is affected by intrinsic parameters of a light field camera
significantly. In the paper, we propose a multi-projection-center (MPC) model
with 6 intrinsic parameters to characterize light field cameras based on
traditional two-parallel-plane (TPP) representation. The MPC model can
generally parameterize light field in different imaging formations, including
conventional and focused light field cameras. By the constraints of 4D ray and
3D geometry, a 3D projective transformation is deduced to describe the
relationship between geometric structure and the MPC coordinates. Based on the
MPC model and projective transformation, we propose a calibration algorithm to
verify our light field camera model. Our calibration method includes a
close-form solution and a non-linear optimization by minimizing re-projection
errors. Experimental results on both simulated and real scene data have
verified the performance of our algorithm.

A second emission enhancement in warm coronal extreme-ultraviolet (EUV) lines
(about 2-7 MK) during some solar flares is known as the EUV late phase. Imaging
observations confirm that the late phase emission originates from a set of
longer or higher loops than the main flare loops. Nevertheless, some questions
remain controversial: What is the relationship between these two loop systems?
What is the heating source of late phase emission, a heating accompany the main
phase heating or occuring quite later? In this paper, we present clear evidence
for heating source in a late-phase solar flare: magnetic reconnection of
overlying field in a quadrupolar magnetic configuration. The event is triggered
by an erupted core structure that eventually leads to a coronal mass ejection
(CME). Cusp feature and its shrinkage motion high in the late-phase emission
region are the manifestation of the later phase reconnection following the main
flare reconnection. Using the enthalpy-based thermal evolution of loops (EBTEL)
model, we reasonably reproduce the late-phase emissions in some EUV lines. We
suggest that a continuous additional heating is responsible for the appearance
of the elongated EUV late phase.

In the second half of the last century the "frozen star" or "collapsed star"
models of black hole formation were largely abandoned. The result is that later
models appear to either assume that black hole event horizons already exist
(such as in Hawking's 1975 paper on radiation from black holes), bypass the
relative frames altogether (such as in Mirabel's 2017 paper on the formation of
stellar black holes), or use coordinate systems that essentially ignore the
remote viewer's point of view (Kruskal 1960).
  This paper attempts to re-establish the concept of a "nascent black hole" as
the correct approach for modelling black holes from remote reference frames. It
uses, and only needs to use, Schwarzschild metrics and presents some example
scenarios to demonstrate the concepts through worked examples. Alternatives
such as Eddington-Finkelstein coordinates and Penrose's local collapsing frames
are not disputed; the Schwarzschild metrics must still be valid outside the
event horizon and this paper is largely concerned with "remote viewers" for
various meanings of "remote".
  For most astrophysical or cosmological questions the difference between
"actual" and "nascent" black holes is largely irrelevant as they behave
similarly for most practical purposes. However the difference becomes critical
for effects that require an event horizon to interact with the remote viewer,
such as Hawking-Zel'dovich radiation and the related information problems
outlined by Susskind. That is: if there are no possible observable paths from a
nascent event horizon to any remote viewer then there also cannot be any
evaporation to the remote viewer

The secondary of the famous young binary RW Aur is much less studied than the
primary. To compensate this shortcoming, we present here the results of
UBVRIJHK photometric, VRI polarimetric and optical spectral observations of RW
Aur B. The star demonstrates chaotic brightness variations in the optical band
with irregular short (~ 1 day) dimmings with an amplitude $\Delta V$ up to
$1.3^{\rm m}.$ The dimmings are accompanied with an increase in the linear
polarization (up to 3 per cent in the I band), presumably due to the scattering
of the stellar radiation by dust in the circumstellar disc that means that RW
Aur B can be classified as a UX Ori type star. We concluded that the observed
excess emission at $\lambda \lesssim 0.45$ $\mu$m and longward $\approx$ 2
$\mu$m as well as a variability of fluxes and profiles of HI, HeI and NaI D
emission lines are due to the accretion process. At the same time, emission
components of Ca II lines indicate that RW Aur B has a powerful chromosphere.
Assuming the solar elemental abundances, we found the following parameters of
the star: $T_{\rm eff} = 4100-4200$ K, $A_{\rm V}=0.6 \pm 0.1$ (out of the
dimming events), $L_* \approx 0.6$ $L_\odot,$ $R_* \approx 1.5$ $R_\odot,$
$M\approx 0.85$ M$_\odot,$ $\dot M_{\rm acc}<5\times 10^{-9}$ M$_\odot$
yr$^{-1}.$ Finally, we discuss possible reasons for the different levels of the
accretion activity of RW Aur binary components and present arguments in favour
of the fact that they are gravitationally bound.

We investigate the transverse momentum ($p_{\rm T}$) spectra of identified
hadrons in minimum-bias proton-proton (pp) collisions at a centre-of-mass
energy ($\sqrt{s}$) of 0.9, 2.76, 5.02, 7 and 13 TeV in the framework of
Tsallis-blast wave (TBW) model. It is found that the model describes well the
particle spectra up to 10 GeV/c. The radial flow ($\langle \beta \rangle$)
increases with the collision energy. The degrees of non-equilibrium ($q$) and
the Tsallis temperature parameter ($T$) show a similar behaviour, but with a
much weaker trend. With this dependence of the freeze-out parameters on the
collision energy, we evaluate $\langle \beta \rangle$, $T$ and $q$ in pp
collisions at $\sqrt{s}=$ 8 and 14 TeV and predict the particle spectra at
these two energies. Moreover, in order to investigate the multiplicity
dependence of the freeze-out parameters, the TBW model is extended to the
spectra at different charged-particle multiplicity classes in pp collisions at
$\sqrt{s}=$ 7 and 13 TeV. It is observed that at both energies the radial flow
increases with the multiplicity while the degree of non-equilibrium shows an
opposite behaviour, which is similar to that observed in proton-nucleus (pA)
and nucleus-nucleus (AA) collisions at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC)
energies. However, the Tsallis temperature parameter increases with the
multiplicity, which is opposite to the trend in pA and AA collisions. At
similar multiplicities, the radial flow in pp collisions is stronger than those
in pA and AA collisions, indicating that the size of the colliding system has
significant effects on the final state particle dynamics. Finally, we apply an
additional flow correction to the Tsallis temperature parameter and find that
the doppler-corrected temperature parameter almost scales with the multiplicity
in a uniform way, despite the difference in the colliding system and collision
energy.

Given a heterogeneous Gaussian sequence model with unknown mean $\theta \in
\mathbb R^d$ and known covariance matrix $\Sigma =
\operatorname{diag}(\sigma_1^2,\dots, \sigma_d^2)$, we study the signal
detection problem against sparse alternatives, for known sparsity $s$. Namely,
we characterize how large $\epsilon^*>0$ should be, in order to distinguish
with high probability the null hypothesis $\theta=0$ from the alternative
composed of $s$-sparse vectors in $\mathbb R^d$, separated from $0$ in $L^t$
norm ($t \geq 1$) by at least $\epsilon^*$. We find minimax upper and lower
bounds over the minimax separation radius $\epsilon^*$ and prove that they are
always matching. We also derive the corresponding minimax tests achieving these
bounds. Our results reveal new phase transitions regarding the behavior of
$\epsilon^*$ with respect to the level of sparsity, to the $L^t$ metric, and to
the heteroscedasticity profile of $\Sigma$. In the case of the Euclidean (i.e.
$L^2$) separation, we bridge the remaining gaps in the literature.

In the cryptanalysis of stream ciphers and pseudorandom sequences, the
notions of linear, jump, and 2-adic complexity arise naturally to measure the
(non)randomness of a given string. We define an isometry K on F_q^\infty that
is the precise equivalent to Euclid's algorithm over the reals to calculate the
continued fraction expansion of a formal power series. The continued fraction
expansion allows to deduce the linear and jump complexity profiles of the input
sequence. Since K is an isometry, the resulting F_q^\infty-sequence is i.i.d.
for i.i.d. input. Hence the linear and jump complexity profiles may be modelled
via Bernoulli experiments (for F_2: coin tossing), and we can apply the very
precise bounds as collected by Revesz, among others the Law of the Iterated
Logarithm.
  The second topic is the 2-adic span and complexity, as defined by Goresky and
Klapper. We derive again an isometry, this time on the dyadic integers Z_2
which induces an isometry A on F_2}^\infty. The corresponding jump complexity
behaves on average exactly like coin tossing.
  Index terms:
  Formal power series, isometry, linear complexity, jump complexity, 2-adic
complexity, 2-adic span, law of the iterated logarithm, Levy classes, stream
ciphers, pseudorandom sequences

Let $E$ be a holomorphic vector bundle. Let $\theta$ be a Higgs field, that
is a holomorphic section of $End(E)\otimes\Omega^{1,0}_X$ satisfying
$\theta^2=0$. Let $h$ be a pluriharmonic metric of the Higgs bundle
$(E,\theta)$. The tuple $(E,\theta,h)$ is called a harmonic bundle.
  Let $X$ be a complex manifold, and $D$ be a normal crossing divisor of $X$.
In this paper, we study the harmonic bundle $(E,\theta,h)$ over $X-D$. We
regard $D$ as the singularity of $(E,\theta,h)$, and we are particularly
interested in the asymptotic behaviour of the harmonic bundle around $D$. We
will see that it is similar to the asymptotic behaviour of complex variation of
polarized Hodge structures, when the harmonic bundle is tame and nilpotent with
the trivial parabolic structure. For example, we prove constantness of general
monodromy weight filtrations, compatibility of the filtrations, norm estimates,
and the purity theorem.
  For that purpose, we will obtain a limiting mixed twistor structure from a
tame nilpotent harmonic bundle with trivial parabolic structure, on a punctured
disc. It is a partial solution of a conjecture of Simpson.

We present a detailed analysis of the local evolution of 206 Lagrangian
Volumes (LVs) selected at high redshift around galaxy seeds, identified in a
large-volume $\Lambda$ cold dark matter ($\Lambda$CDM) hydrodynamical
simulation. The LVs have a mass range of $1 - 1500 \times 10^{10} M_\odot$. We
follow the dynamical evolution of the density field inside these initially
spherical LVs from $z=10$ up to $z_{\rm low} = 0.05$, witnessing highly
non-linear, anisotropic mass rearrangements within them, leading to the
emergence of the local cosmic web (CW). These mass arrangements have been
analysed in terms of the reduced inertia tensor $I_{ij}^r$, focusing on the
evolution of the principal axes of inertia and their corresponding
eigendirections, and paying particular attention to the times when the
evolution of these two structural elements declines. In addition, mass and
component effects along this process have also been investigated. We have found
that deformations are led by dark matter dynamics and they transform most of
the initially spherical LVs into prolate shapes, i.e. filamentary structures.
An analysis of the individual freezing-out time distributions for shapes and
eigendirections shows that first most of the LVs fix their three axes of
symmetry (like a skeleton) early on, while accretion flows towards them still
continue. Very remarkably, we have found that more massive LVs fix their
skeleton earlier on than less massive ones. We briefly discuss the
astrophysical implications our findings could have, including the galaxy
mass-morphology relation and the effects on the galaxy-galaxy merger parameter
space, among others.

We study the dark-time decay of the retrieval efficiency for light stored in
a Rydberg state in an ultracold gas of $^{87}$Rb atoms based on
electromagnetically induced transparency (EIT). Using low atomic density to
avoid dephasing caused by atom-atom interactions, we measure a $1/e$ time of 30
$\mu$s for the $80S$ state in free expansion. One of the dominant limitations
is the combination of photon recoil and thermal atomic motion at 0.2 $\mu$K. If
the 1064-nm dipole trap is left on, then the $1/e$ time is reduced to 13
$\mu$s, in agreement with a model taking differential light shifts and
gravitational sag into account. To characterize how coherent the retrieved
light is, we overlap it with reference light and measure the visibility $V$ of
the resulting interference pattern, obtaining $V> 90\%$ for short dark time.
Our experimental work is accompanied by a detailed model for the dark-time
decay of the retrieval efficiency of light stored in atomic ensembles. The
model is generally applicable for photon storage in Dicke states, such as in
EIT with $\Lambda$-type or ladder-type level schemes and in
Duan-Lukin-Cirac-Zoller single-photon sources. The model includes a treatment
of the dephasing caused by thermal atomic motion combined with net photon
recoil, as well as the influence of trapping potentials. It takes into account
that the signal light field is typically not a plane wave. The model maps the
retrieval efficiency to single-atom properties and shows that the retrieval
efficiency is related to the decay of fringe visibility in Ramsey spectroscopy
and to the spatial first-order coherence function of the gas.

Superconducting metamaterials combine the advantages of low-loss, large
inductance (with the addition of kinetic inductance), and extreme tunability
compared to their normal metal counterparts. Therefore, they allow realization
of compact designs operating at low frequencies. We have recently developed
radio frequency (RF) metamaterials with a high loaded quality factor and an
electrical size as small as $\sim$$\lambda$658, ($\lambda$ is the free space
wavelength) by using Nb thin films. The RF metamaterial is composed of truly
planar spirals patterned with lithographic techniques. Linear transmission
characteristics of these metamaterials show robust Lorentzian resonant peaks in
the sub- 100 MHz frequency range below the $T_c$ of Nb. Though Nb is a
non-magnetic material, the circulating currents in the spirals generated by RF
signals produce a strong magnetic response, which can be tuned sensitively
either by temperature or magnetic field thanks to the superconducting nature of
the design. We have also observed strong nonlinearity and meta-stable jumps in
the transmission data with increasing RF input power until the Nb is driven
into the normal state. We discuss the factors modifying the induced magnetic
response from single and 1-D arrays of spirals in the light of numerical
simulations.

A scalable quantum information processing architecture based on silicon
metal-oxide-semiconductor technology is presented, combining quantum hardware
elements from planar and 3D silicon-on-insulator technologies. This
architecture is expressed in the ``unit cell'' approach, where tiling cells in
two dimensions and allowing inter-cellular nearest-neighbour interactions makes
the architecture compatible with the surface code for fault tolerant quantum
computation. The architecture utilises global control methods, substantially
reducing processor complexity with scale: Single-qubit control is achieved
using globally applied spin-resonance techniques and two-qubit interactions are
mediated by large quantum dots. Further, a solution to device variation is
proposed through integration of electronics for individual trimming of quantum
dot voltage references. Such a combined set of solutions addresses several
major barriers to scaling quantum machines within completely silicon based
architectures.

Recent Alzheimer's disease (AD) patient studies have focused on retinal
analysis, as the retina is the only part of the central nervous system which
can be imaged non-invasively by optical methods. However as this is a
relatively new approach, the occurrence and role of pathological features such
as retinal layer thinning, extracellular amyloid beta (A$\beta$) accumulation
and vascular changes is still debated. Animal models of AD are therefore often
used in attempts to understand the disease. In this work, both eyes of 24
APP/PS1 transgenic mice (age: 45-104 weeks) and 15 age-matched wildtype
littermates were imaged by a custom-built multi-contrast optical coherence
tomography (OCT) system. The system provided a combination of standard
reflectivity data, polarization-sensitive data and OCT angiograms. This
tri-fold contrast provided qualitative and quantitative information on retinal
layer thickness and structure, presence of hyper-reflective foci, phase
retardation abnormalities and retinal vasculature. While abnormal structural
properties and phase retardation signals were observed in the retinas, the
observations were very similar in transgenic and control mice. At the end of
the experiment, retinas and brains were harvested from a subset of the mice (14
transgenic, 7 age-matched control) in order to compare the in vivo results to
histological analysis, and to quantify the cortical A$\beta$ plaque load.

Subdominant contributions to the entanglement entropy of quantum fields
include logarithmic corrections to the area law characterized by universal
coefficients that are independent of the ultraviolet regulator and capture
detailed information on the geometry around the entangling surface. We
determine two universal coefficients of the entanglement entropy for a massive
scalar field in a static closed universe $\mathbb{R} \times \mathbb{S}^3$
perturbatively and verify the results numerically. The first coefficient
describes a well known generic correction to the area law independent of the
geometry of the entangling surface and background. The second coefficient
describes a curvature-dependent universal term with a nontrivial dependence on
the intrinsic and extrinsic geometries of the entangling surface and curvature
of the background. The numerical calculations confirm the analytical results to
a high accuracy. The first and second universal coefficients are determined
numerically with a relative error with respect to the analytical values of the
orders $10^{-4}$ and $10^{-2}$, respectively.

The coherence resource theory needs to study the operational value and
efficiency which can be broadly formulated as the question: when can one
coherent state be converted into another under incoherent operations. We answer
this question completely for one-mode continuous-variable systems by
characterizing conversion of coherent Gaussian states under incoherent Gaussian
operations in terms of their first and second moments. The no-go theorem of
purification of coherent Gaussian states is also built. The structure of
incoherent Gaussian operations of two-mode continuous-variable systems is
discussed further and is applied to coherent conversion for pure Gaussian
states with standard second moments. The standard second moments are images of
all second moments under local linear unitary Bogoliubov operations. As
concrete applications, we obtain some peculiarities of a Gaussian system: (1)
There does not exist a maximally coherent Gaussian state which can generate all
coherent Gaussian states; (2) The conversion between pure Gaussian states is
reversible; (3) The coherence of input pure state and the coherence of output
pure state are equal.

The energy flux patterns of inverse Goos-Hanchen (GH) shift around the
interface between air and negatively refractive photonic crystal (NRPhC) with
different surface terminations is investigated. Results show that NRPhC
exhibits inverse GH shift in TM and TE polarization, and the localization and
pattern of energy flux differ in TM and TE polarizations and are strongly
affected by surface termination. This is different to the condition of negative
permittivity materials (i.e., metal), which only presents inverse GH shift in
TM polarization. In the case of TE polarization, the energy flux pattern
exhibits the flux of backward wave whose localization changes from the surface
to inside of NRPhC with the variation of surface termination. In the case of TM
polarization, the energy flux pattern is always confined within the surface of
NRPhC, whereas its pattern changes from the flux of backward wave to vortices
at the surface of NRPhC, which is different to the energy flux of TM
polarization of metal. By properly truncating the surface of NRPhC we can
control the magnitude of inverse GH shift for TM and TE polarized light.

The paper considers autonomous rendezvous maneuver and proximity operations
of two spacecraft in presence of obstacles. A strategy that combines guidance
and control algorithms is analyzed. The proposed closed-loop system is able to
guarantee a safe path in a real environment, as well as robustness with respect
to external disturbances and dynamic obstacles. The guidance strategy exploits
a suitably designed Artificial Potential Field (APF), while the controller
relies on Sliding Mode Control (SMC), for both position and attitude tracking
of the spacecraft. As for the position control, two different first order SMC
methods are considered, namely the component-wise and the simplex-based control
techniques. The proposed integrated guidance and control strategy is validated
by extensive simulations performed with a six degree-of-freedom (DOF) orbital
simulator and appears suitable for real-time control with minimal on-board
computational effort. Fuel consumption and control effort are evaluated,
including different update frequencies of the closed-loop software.

This work investigates the evolution of the distribution of charged particles
(cosmic rays) due to the mechanism of stochastic turbulent acceleration (STA)
in presence of small-scale turbulence with a mean magnetic field. STA is
usually modelled as a biased random walk process in the momentum space of the
non-thermal particles. This results in an advection-diffusion type transport
equation for the non-thermal particle distribution function. Under quasilinear
approximation, and by assuming a turbulent spectrum with single scale injection
at sub-gyroscale, we find that the Fokker-Planck diffusion coefficients
$D_{\gamma\gamma}$ and $D_{\mu\mu}$ scale with the Lorentz factor $\gamma$ as:
$D_{\gamma\gamma}\propto\gamma^{-2/3}$ and $D_{\mu\mu}\propto\gamma^{-8/3}$.
Furthermore, with the calculated transport coefficients, we numerically solve
the advection-diffusion type transport equation for the non-thermal particles.
We demonstrate the interplay of various mircophysical processes such as STA,
synchrotron loss and particle escape on the particle distribution by
systematically varying the parameters of the problem.

Optimal deployment of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) as communication relays
is studied for fixed-rate variable-power systems. The considered setup is a set
of ground transmitters (GTs) wishing to communicate with a set of ground
receivers (GRs) through the UAVs. Each GT-GR pair communicates through only one
selected UAV and have no direct link. Two different UAV selection scenarios are
studied: In centralized selection, a decision center assigns an optimal UAV
depending on the locations of all terminals. In distributed selection, a GT
selects its relaying UAV using only the local knowledge of its distances to the
UAVs. For both selection scenarios, the optimal tradeoff between the UAV and GT
power consumptions are determined using tools from quantization theory.
Specifically, the two extremal regimes of one UAV and very large number of UAVs
are analyzed for a path loss exponent of $2$. Numerical optimization of UAV
locations are also discussed. Simulations are provided to confirm the
analytical findings.

We consider the radiation-hydrodynamical formation of a dusty wall in the
circumnuclear regions of the Circinus Seyfert 2 galaxy. We focus on the
radiative flux force due to the circumnuclear starburst, the nuclear starburst
and the active galactic nucleus (AGN), and analyze the equilibrium
configuration and stability of the dusty gas in the circumnuclear regions. The
mass distributions used to model the gravitational fields are determined by the
observed rotation velocities of the stars and of the gas. Moreover, by using
the simple stellar evolution in the circumnuclear starburst, the bolometric
luminosity of the starburst is estimated as a function of time. As a result, it
is shown that the radiation force by the circumnuclear starburst does play an
important role to build up a radiatively-supported dusty wall of $\la$ 100 pc,
which obscures the circumnuclear regions with $A_V$ of a few magnitudes. It is
also found that the age of a circumnuclear starburst when the circumnuclear
regions are enshrouded by the dusty wall is constrained to be $\la 10^8$ yr,
which is consistent with the age observationally inferred for the starburst, $4
10^7$ yr $-1.5 10^8$ yr.

A permutation $\sigma = [\sigma_1, \dots, \sigma_n] \in S_n$ is termed a *cylindrical king permutation* if $|\sigma_{i+1} - \sigma_i| > 1$ for all $1 \leq i \leq n-1$, and additionally $|\sigma_1 - \sigma_n| > 1$. This name arises from the visual interpretation of such permutations as placing $n$ kings on an $n \times n$ chessboard where each row and each column contains exactly one king, no two kings attack each other, and with the twist that a king may leave one row and re-enter at its beginning.

In a prior work, we studied a broader class called "king permutations," which only require the first condition—namely, adjacent values in the permutation differ by more than one. This set forms a poset under the standard containment order for permutations.

In this paper, we analyze the sub-poset consisting of cylindrical king permutations and explore its structure. We focus on those permutations whose downsets are as extensive as possible in the higher-tier elements. By modifying the Manhattan distance associated with the graphical representation of a permutation and adapting it to the cylindrical setup, we establish a criterion for identifying $k$-prolific permutations. A key finding is that the largest possible gap between any two permutations in this poset is exactly 4.

A Monte Carlo filter is introduced, inspired by a particle-based, time-discretized approach to the Kushner-Stratonovich (KS) nonlinear filtering equation. Central to this method is an additive update rule resembling a gain term, designed to mimic the innovation integral in the KS equation. This update is implemented via an annealing-based iterative process, aiming to transform the observation-prediction discrepancy at each time step into a zero-mean Brownian increment aligned with measurement noise. This differs from the multiplicative weight updates commonly used in particle filters, which are prone to weight collapse in finite-environment implementations. An analysis of the prior error bounds in this scheme is conducted. Numerical results from testing the proposed filter—alongside a few alternative approaches—on various nonlinear dynamic system identification and target tracking tasks indicate significant improvements in both convergence and accuracy.

Metallic LaAlO₃/SrTiO₃ (LAO/STO) interfaces have garnered significant interest, yet the connection between electron mobility and sheet electron density, $n_s$, remains unclear. We present a straightforward formula linking the three-dimensional electron density near the interface, $n_{3D}$, to $n_s$, showing that the mobility in LAO/STO interfaces follows the same dependence on $n_{3D}$ as in heavily doped bulk SrTiO₃. It is well established that undoped bulk STO exhibits strong compensation, with a background concentration of donors and acceptors approximately $N \simeq 5 \times 10^{18}~\rm{cm^{-3}}$. In doped bulk STO where $n_{3D} < N$, scattering is dominated by these background impurities, so mobility in such cases mirrors that of bulk material. In contrast, when $n_{3D} > N$, mobility drops due to scattering from the intentionally added donors. In LAO/STO, the polar catastrophe generates electrons but does not produce an equal number of randomly distributed donors, so one would expect higher mobility. The observation that mobility remains identical suggests the current interpretation of the polar catastrophe mechanism in LAO/STO needs to be reevaluated.

Semiconductor nanocrystals can be produced using simple, scalable solution methods, and their ability to emit light at adjustable wavelengths has been successfully applied in areas such as biological labeling and optoelectronic devices. However, their use in optical amplification and lasing has remained hindered by the extremely high pump power densities needed. Here, we demonstrate that colloidal nanoplatelets (NPLs) achieve amplified spontaneous emission (ASE) with threshold pump fluences as low as 6 μJ/cm² and gains up to 600 cm⁻¹—representing a fourfold enhancement over previously reported results for colloidal nanocrystals. Notably, gain saturation happens at pump fluences that are two orders of magnitude above the ASE threshold. This outstanding performance stems from their large optical cross-sections, suppressed Auger recombination, and narrow collective emission linewidth. By combining the optical properties of quantum wells with a colloidal platform, nanoplatelets enable the development of efficient, solution-based lasers.

We introduce a randomized approach for solving linear programs with many columns and few constraints. Because it's impractical to consider all columns explicitly, these problems are typically addressed using column generation—a technique that can still face computational difficulties due to the difficulty of solving the associated subproblem in practice. Rather than adding columns one by one, as in traditional column generation, our method samples a set of columns based on a user-defined random scheme and then solves the resulting linear program using only the selected columns. Although prior work has explored sampling columns—equivalently, sampling constraints in the dual—to solve large-scale problems, this paper provides a probabilistic upper bound on the optimality gap, converging at a rate of $1 / \sqrt{K}$, where $K$ is the number of sampled columns. This bound compares the solution to the optimal value of a related linear program—what we call the distributional counterpart—whose gap we analyze and establish conditions under which it remains small. Numerical experiments are conducted in two settings: the cutting-stock problem and nonparametric choice model estimation, showing the method’s effectiveness.

Integrated sensing, computation, and communication (ISCC) is emerging as a key enabler for next-generation beyond 5G networks. In such systems, the growing competition between sensing operations—needed for ambient intelligence—and computation demands from mobile devices presents a significant challenge. To tackle this, we introduce an efficient sensing architecture featuring a new action detection component that identifies whether the sensed object is stationary. This capability helps reduce unnecessary computational load. We then evaluate the sensing performance of this framework, using the sampling theorem to establish its theoretical effectiveness. A sensing accuracy optimization problem is formulated under strict quality-of-service (QoS) constraints. To resolve it, we develop an optimal resource allocation method that minimizes computational resource usage while assigning the remainder to sensing tasks. Additionally, we derive a practical threshold selection rule. Comparative analysis shows that our framework outperforms traditional approaches, underscoring its practical value. Finally, real-world action recognition experiments conducted using the USRP B210 platform validate our performance models, and comprehensive testing confirms the superior performance of our solution over existing benchmark methods.

This paper conducts a theoretical investigation into the eigenmodes of a misaligned ring laser cavity, where one or more arms contain an inhomogeneous medium. Beginning with a general formulation of the radiation properties for a ring resonator featuring arbitrary inhomogeneities in both the medium and mirror properties—within the paraxial approximation—the study focuses on a lens-like resonator model. In this model, the real and imaginary parts of the refractive index, along with the phase and amplitude modifications introduced by the mirrors, vary quadratically with the transverse coordinates. Closed-form expressions are derived for the eigenfrequency and spatial profile of the radiation, expressed in terms of the coefficients of Hermite-Gaussian functions that describe the complex amplitude at the mirrors. The key findings are presented as linear relationships between the positions of the resonator mode axis across the mirrors and the mirror misalignments—such as small translations and tilts. The coefficients of these linear dependencies are explicitly determined for a three-mirror cavity, using a specialized approach grounded in two fundamental principles: (1) the independence of frequency shifts from the sequence of misalignments—a property rooted in energy-based resonator analysis, which is extended and refined here; and (2) the geometric symmetry of the cavity structure. These computed coefficients enable precise control over the radiation behavior in response to mirror deviations, supporting detailed assessments of output beam stability and sensitivity to subtle changes in cavity alignment.

Molecular line observations with various tracers are frequently employed to study the kinematic properties of molecular clouds. Yet, velocity dispersion measurements derived from different lines—especially within the same region—often show discrepancies. The underlying causes remain unclear due to several observational biases. In this study, we examine the sources of these biases by generating synthetic position-position-velocity cubes from a set of self-gravitating magnetohydrodynamic simulations of molecular clouds. We then compare the linewidths obtained from these synthetic observations to the actual values in the simulations. Our results show that differences in measured linewidths across tracers arise from a combination of density-dependent excitation, where higher-density-sensitive tracers probe more compact regions with lower velocity dispersions, opacity-induced broadening—particularly pronounced in highly optically thick species like CO—and limitations in spatial and spectral resolution, which truncate the emission line wings. We find that, at a fixed signal-to-noise ratio, three widely used tracers—the J=4→3 transition of CO, the J=1→0 transition of C¹⁸O, and the (1,1) inversion line of NH₃—offer the best balance among these systematic effects. These tracers yield velocity dispersion estimates that are accurate to within about 10% of the true kinematics, irrespective of the cloud’s magnetic field strength, evolutionary stage, or the orientation of the line of sight relative to the magnetic field.

Valued constraint satisfaction problems (VCSPs) are discrete optimisation problems where the objective function takes values in $(\mathbb{Q} \cup \{\infty\})$ and is expressed as a sum of functions of fixed arity. In the setting of Boolean surjective VCSPs, variables assume values in $D = \{0,1\}$, and the goal is to find an assignment that uses both labels from $D$. Prominent examples include the global Min-Cut problem in graphs and the Minimum Distance problem in coding theory.

We prove a dichotomy theorem, providing a full complexity classification for Boolean surjective VCSPs with respect to exact solvability. This extends prior results by Creignou and Hébrard on dichotomies for $\{0,\infty\}$-valued constraint languages—known to correspond to surjective decision CSPs. Additionally, for the maximisation variant of $\mathbb{Q}_{\geq 0}$-valued surjective VCSPs, we establish a dichotomy concerning approximation hardness.

In contrast to the non-surjective case, a new tractable class of constraint languages emerges in the surjective setting—one that is trivially intractable without the surjective requirement. This class possesses a distinctive mathematical structure, connected to downsets and upsets. Our primary contribution is the identification of this class and showing it resides at the threshold between tractable and intractable instances. A central element of our proof is a polynomial-time algorithm that enumerates all near-optimal solutions for a generalised Min-Cut problem—a result of potential independent value.

Video object segmentation (VOS) involves identifying and segmenting multiple objects across each frame of a video. In semi-supervised setups, the initial mask for each object is available during testing. Under the one-shot paradigm, existing VOS methods train a segmentation model independently for each object using the provided mask. However, the VOS research community has recently concluded that such test-time fine-tuning—especially its effects on inference speed—is impractical. To improve efficiency, we propose efficient One-Shot Video Object Segmentation (e-OSVOS). Unlike conventional VOS methods, e-OSVOS separates object detection from segmentation, relying on a modified Mask R-CNN to predict only localized masks. We optimize both inference time and accuracy without requiring extensive or manually tuned hyperparameter settings. To this effect, we perform meta-learning to determine model initialization and learning rates at test time, with learning rates tailored per neuron to ensure effective adaptation. Additionally, we introduce online adaptation to counteract performance drops across video sequences by continuously refining the model using prior frame predictions, facilitated by frame-to-frame bounding box propagation. e-OSVOS achieves top-tier results on DAVIS 2016, DAVIS 2017, and YouTube-VOS for one-shot methods while significantly reducing test runtime. The code is available at https://github.com/dvl-tum/e-osvos.

A Dirac semimetal is a quantum phase characterized by excitations governed by the relativistic Dirac equation. In the massless limit, parity-time symmetry ensures that the Dirac fermions in momentum space—composed of two Weyl fermions with opposite chirality—are non-chiral. Drawing an analogy with flavor symmetry in particle physics, we propose a massless Dirac-like theory that unifies two Weyl fermions of the same chirality, based on an assumed SU(2) isospin symmetry, which remains invariant under spatial rotations swapping the two fields. Remarkably, this symmetry is hidden in certain spin-1/2 solid-state systems with minimal spin-orbit coupling, where spin and lattice degrees of freedom are decoupled. As a result, the predicted quasiparticle—termed the flavor Weyl fermion—cannot be described within the standard magnetic space group framework. This four-fold degenerate flavor Weyl fermion exhibits linear energy dispersion and a Chern number of 2, giving rise to a topologically stable network of Fermi arcs spanning the entire Brillouin zone. For experimental realization, we identify CoNb₃S₆—a transition metal chalcogenide with experimentally verified collinear antiferromagnetic ordering—as a promising candidate for a flavor Weyl semimetal under the approximation of negligible spin-orbit coupling. Our findings reveal a solid-state manifestation of flavor symmetry in magnetic systems, opening new pathways for observing exotic quantum phenomena in materials.

This paper introduces a new framework for uncertainty quantification in scientific machine learning—referred to as IB-UQ—applied to tasks such as deep neural network regression and neural operator learning (e.g., DeepONet). Starting with the General Incompressible-Flow Networks (GIN) model, we first learn a broad probability distribution from noisy observations. Subsequently, guided by the information bottleneck principle, we derive a stochastic mapping from input features to a latent representation that enables accurate output predictions. To make the optimization feasible, we construct a practical variational approximation of the IB objective using a normalizing flow reparameterization, allowing efficient training via stochastic gradient descent. By explicitly modeling the latent representation variables, IB-UQ not only outputs the expected prediction but also quantifies its uncertainty—both mean and variance. Unlike conventional deep learning regression approaches or deterministic DeepONet, the proposed method is robust to noisy training data and delivers precise, trustworthy predictions even for previously unseen, noisy inputs. The effectiveness of the IB-UQ framework is illustrated through multiple case studies, including regression of discontinuous functions, real-world dataset fitting, and learning nonlinear operators governing diffusion-reaction partial differential equations.

In the consensus halving problem, we have n agents who evaluate a one-dimensional interval $[0,1]$. The objective is to divide this interval into at most $n+1$ segments—using no more than n cuts—such that the resulting two subsets are valued equally by all agents. The existence of such a division follows from the Borsuk-Ulam theorem. We focus on computing an approximate solution when the agents' valuations are represented by distribution functions evaluated through algebraic circuits. Here, an approximation means finding a point that is within $\varepsilon$ of an exact solution, known as a strong approximation. We prove that this computational task is polynomial-time reducible to approximating a solution to the Borsuk-Ulam search problem, where the function governing the search is computed via an algebraic circuit.

The Borsuk-Ulam search problem is the foundational problem associated with the complexity class BU. We introduce a new complexity class, BBU, to formalize a different computational perspective on the Borsuk-Ulam theorem. We analyze the relationship between BU and BBU, and establish several structural properties for these classes, as well as for the class FIXP.

We perform an analysis of radial velocity distributions in dark matter haloes using cosmological N-body simulations, focusing on the lowest-order even moments—specifically, dispersion and kurtosis. For ten massive haloes within the simulation volume, we model their density profiles using the NFW profile, defined by virial mass and concentration. We also compute the velocity anisotropy parameter, which we find to be moderately radial and grows with radial distance from the halo center. The radial velocity dispersion exhibits a distinct profile, peaking at some radius, while the radial kurtosis decreases with distance, starting from near-Gaussian values at the core. This confirms that dark matter haloes exhibit intrinsically non-Gaussian, flat-topped velocity distributions. The simulated radial velocity moments are accurately captured by solutions to the Jeans equations, using the halo parameters—specifically the measured anisotropy—determined directly from the simulations. Furthermore, we examine the radial velocity moments in a composite cluster composed of ten haloes, extending out to ten virial radii. In this range, velocity dispersion systematically declines to the level of the background, while kurtosis rises from below to above the Gaussian threshold of 3, indicating a transition from a flat-topped to a sharply peaked velocity distribution compared to a Gaussian. This shift suggests a dominance of ordered motion with only small dispersion. We illustrate this transition by presenting the velocity distribution across several radial bins in the composite system.

Users on social media interpret content through the lens of context, with emotional tone playing a key role in shaping their responses. This has led to a flood of emotionally charged and opinion-driven content across platforms. Researchers are constantly exploring new methods and tools to harness this data more effectively. Yet, challenges persist due to the complexity and variability of the data, the diversity of platforms, and the ever-changing nature of user behavior. Accurately capturing a user’s emotional state before they post a comment remains a difficult task. This study presents a method for semi-supervised, multilingual emotion detection by leveraging the overlap between Facebook reaction patterns and textual content. Using this emotion detection system, we investigate whether emotional states and user behavior can contribute to sarcasm detection. A dataset comprising more than 1 million English and Chinese comments from over 62,000 public Facebook posts has been gathered and processed. Experimental results demonstrate promising performance in detecting sarcasm using emotional and behavioral indicators.

A central issue in statistical mechanics is whether the microcanonical entropy should be defined as the logarithm of the phase space volume—commonly called the volume entropy or Gibbs entropy—or as the logarithm of the density of states, known as the surface entropy or Boltzmann entropy. Rather than arbitrarily selecting one definition and examining its implications, as is typically done, we take a bottom-up approach. Starting from two foundational principles of thermodynamics—(i) the second law in the context of quasi-static processes, which asserts that $\delta Q/T$ is an exact differential, and (ii) the ideal gas law, $PV = k_B NT$—we derive the entropy expression within the microcanonical framework. The first principle demands that entropy be a function of the phase volume $\Omega$. The second principle uniquely identifies the logarithmic function among all possible forms. Thus, this derivation leads exclusively to $S = k_B \ln \Omega$, the Gibbs entropy. Any alternative—such as the Boltzmann entropy—fails to satisfy these principles and results in inconsistencies. We demonstrate this using a standard model: a large ensemble of non-interacting spins in a magnetic field. Under these conditions, the Boltzmann entropy fails to correctly predict the magnetization, even in the thermodynamic limit. The robustness of the Gibbs entropy and the known flaws of the Boltzmann entropy offer strong grounds for rejecting the latter.

Molecular and atomic-scale imaging required breakthroughs in electron and scanning probe microscopy to overcome diffraction-limited resolution. Subsequent advances, such as nano-infrared experiments and pico-cavity tip-enhanced Raman spectroscopy, showed that visible-light radiation can surpass these limits by exploiting the near-field via scanning probe tips. In this work, we demonstrate that even higher resolution—down to 1 nm—is achievable using scanning microwave imaging microscopy with a wavelength of 0.1 m. We use twisted bilayer graphene as a model system: not only is it central to recent research due to emergent correlated phenomena like superconductivity, but it also allows us to precisely control the Moiré superlattice modulation, ranging from below one nanometer to tens of nanometers. By examining variations in tip-sample distance, we confirm that the unprecedented resolution of 10⁸ in probe-to-pattern fidelity is realized through liquid immersion microscopy and highly refined nanoscale force management—specifically, control over the meniscus of water at the nanoscale.

This study aims to formulate the averaged governing equations for non-degenerated oscillatory flows where the mean and oscillatory velocity components are comparable in magnitude. We derive these averaged equations for three physical cases: a passive scalar, a passive vector quantity (such as a magnetic field in kinematic magnetohydrodynamics), and vortex dynamics. The asymptotic analysis is based on the small parameter $1/\sigma$, the inverse of the dimensionless frequency. Our method integrates the two-timing technique, distinguished limits, and commutator algebra to streamline computations, yielding recurrence relations for both the averaged and oscillating components of the fields. No additional physical or mathematical assumptions beyond standard ones are introduced, and explicit computations are carried out up to the first three successive approximations (zeroth, first, and second orders). Across all considered cases, the resulting averaged equations share a common structure: Reynolds-stress-type terms (or cross-correlations) are replaced by drift velocities, pseudo-diffusion, and two further terms analogous to Moffatt’s mean-field contributions in turbulent flows. Specifically, the averaged evolution of a passive scalar involves only drift and pseudo-diffusion. In contrast, the averaged equations for passive vector fields and vortex dynamics include two additional mean-field terms beyond pseudo-diffusion. Notably, all mean-field terms—including pseudo-diffusion—are expressed in terms of invariant operators (Lie derivatives) that quantify deviations of certain tensors from their "frozen-in" values. These results provide a foundation for further physical reasoning and applications. Our interpretation implies that pseudo-diffusion has a purely kinematic origin.

This work serves as a continuation of [Oh5] (ArXiv:math.SG/0206092). Its primary goal is to establish an existence theorem for pseudo-holomorphic sections of the mapping cylinder, endowed with energy estimates—crucial for proving the nondegeneracy of a homological invariant pseudo-norm introduced in [Oh4,5]. This existence result also lies at the heart of the author’s recent proof of an optimal energy-capacity inequality in [Oh5]. Here, we present a more general existence theorem than strictly required, focusing on Floer’s perturbed Cauchy-Riemann equations with discontinuous Hamiltonian perturbations and demonstrating the existence of piecewise smooth, finite-energy solutions. The proof is based on a detailed analysis of the product structure in the chain-level Floer homology and a singular degeneration—referred to as adiabatic degeneration—of the perturbed equations. Along the way, we also establish a general energy identity for pseudo-holomorphic sections of the Hamiltonian fibration.

This paper introduces a search-driven partial motion planner designed to produce dynamically feasible paths for car-like robots operating in highly dynamic environments. By constructing a state graph using motion primitives—derived from discretizing both time and control inputs—the planner searches for smooth, safe, and nearly time-optimal trajectories. To enable rapid online execution, we develop an efficient path-searching method that aggregates and prunes motion primitives for faster exploration. Additionally, we present a fast collision detection approach that accounts for the movement of dynamic obstacles. It works by linearizing the relative motion between the robot and obstacles, then determining collisions through point-to-line distance evaluation. Thanks to these optimized search and collision detection mechanisms, the planner can efficiently and safely traverse the state-time space to yield near-time-optimal solutions. Experimental results demonstrate that the method generates valid trajectories in less than a millisecond while achieving a higher success rate than existing approaches, clearly highlighting its effectiveness.

In a peer-to-peer inference framework, each node can perform local reasoning and optionally query nearby peers—those sharing common vocabulary terms. This paper focuses on such systems where each peer's local theory consists of propositional clauses based on its individual vocabulary. A defining feature of these systems is that the complete global theory—the combined set of all local theories—is not accessible to any peer, unlike in partitioned reasoning models. The key contribution of this work is the development of DeCA, the first consequence-finding algorithm designed specifically for peer-to-peer environments. DeCA operates incrementally and continuously, deriving consequences starting from nearby peers and progressively expanding to more distant ones. We establish a sufficient condition on the acquaintance graph that ensures the algorithm’s completeness. Additionally, we demonstrate how this distributed reasoning model can be applied to Semantic Web applications via the Somewhere semantic peer-to-peer data management system. Finally, we conduct an experimental evaluation of the scalability of our proposed peer-to-peer infrastructure on networks comprising up to 1000 peers.

Coulomb drag in nanoscale conductors holds both fundamental and practical significance. In this work, we present a theoretical analysis of drag in a double quantum-dot system where one dot is biased as a drive, and the other, unbounded, serves as a drag dot, connected through direct interdot Coulomb coupling. We show that drag arises from charge fluctuations within the driven dot, and illustrate how the quantum noise associated with these fluctuations enables clear distinction from other forms of drag, such as that caused by shot noise in weakly interacting quantum circuits. In the strong-coupling regime characterized by an orbital ("pseudospin") Kondo effect, the drag is primarily due to charge variations stemming from pseudospin-flip cotunneling events. We find that Kondo correlations suppress these pseudospin-flip processes at low bias, leading to reduced drag and a zero-bias peak in the second-order differential transconductance. Moreover, we identify that drag reaches its maximum when the interdot interaction strength equals the coupling to the leads. These results contribute to a deeper understanding of Coulomb drag in quantum dot systems and offer testable predictions across various transport conditions.

Node-link diagrams are commonly used to visualize networks, but despite advanced layout algorithms, they often become cluttered and indistinct—known as "hairball" visualizations—when networks grow complex. This makes detailed exploration difficult, typically requiring aggregation or interactive tools like filtering to maintain usability. Until recently, there has been limited evidence on how network complexity affects visual effectiveness or how visual complexity influences cognitive load. To address this gap, we performed a controlled experiment focused on a topology-sensitive task: identifying the shortest path between two nodes. We evaluated performance across networks ranging from 25 to 175 nodes, varying in density. Our analysis included accuracy, reaction time, subjective evaluations, and physiological signals—EEG, pupil dilation, and heart rate variability—to assess cognitive effort. To our knowledge, this is the first study to incorporate physiological data in network visualization research. Our findings reveal that participants struggle significantly in high-density graphs with over 50 nodes and even in low-density graphs exceeding 100 nodes. EEG data demonstrated clear differences in brain activity between challenging and straightforward tasks. Cognitive load peaked at a specific complexity threshold and then declined, suggesting participants often abandoned the task. We also investigated how overall layout characteristics—such as graph size and edge crossings—and path-specific attributes like length and straightness affected task performance. Results indicate that global layout features exert a stronger influence on difficulty than properties of the shortest path itself.

Space telescopes used for imaging solar hard X-rays capture data in the form of sampled Fourier components of the incoming photon flux. This study focuses on developing an image reconstruction technique that enhances visibility interpolation within the Fourier domain. The proposed method is tested on synthetic visibility data generated using simulation tools developed for the Spectrometer/Telescope for Imaging X-rays (STIX) mission aboard the Solar Orbiter. Additionally, its performance is evaluated on real visibility measurements obtained by the Reuven Ramaty High Energy Solar Spectroscopic Imager (RHESSI). To improve interpolation, we employ an approach based on Variably Scaled Kernels (VSKs), which leverage prior knowledge about the flaring source to enhance features—an application of VSKs to image reconstruction that has not been previously demonstrated. Compared to a prior interpolation-based algorithm for RHESSI data, VSKs deliver substantially improved results, especially for STIX, where Fourier sampling is highly sparse. For RHESSI observations, the VSK method proves particularly effective when flares exhibit narrow, ribbon-like structures or when high-resolution detectors are employed. The approach enables high-fidelity image reconstruction when only limited scattered Fourier data represent the flare’s characteristics and when the visibility surface exhibits strong frequency-domain oscillations.

Supernova neutrinos exhibit unique characteristics that give rise to fascinating physical effects. At their point of emission, their wave packets are incredibly compact—on the order of $\sigma_x \sim 10^{-11}$ cm—leading to a large energy uncertainty, $\sigma_E \sim E$, and a very short coherence length. As they travel toward Earth, the wave packets of individual mass eigenstates expand to macroscopic scales and begin to decohere. Within Earth, these mass states evolve into matter-induced eigenstates and resume oscillations. The coherence length inside Earth becomes roughly on the order of the planet’s radius. In this work, we examine these properties and their implications: (i) We offer refined calculations of the wave packet size. (ii) We analyze the decoherence condition for wave packets that have spatially extended profiles and find that the spread does not alter the decoherence threshold. (iii) We investigate neutrino coherence in a multi-layer medium with abrupt density changes at layer boundaries. In such environments, partial restoration of coherence can occur via a "catch-up" mechanism, effectively extending the coherence length beyond standard estimates. This effect is relevant for supernova neutrinos as they traverse shock fronts in the exploding star or pass through Earth’s core.

We perform numerical simulations to trace the orbital evolution of long-period comets, modeling their journey from the Oort cloud to eventual ejection or disruption. The simulations include the gravitational influence of the Sun, the four giant planets, and the Galactic tide. We also analyze the impact of non-gravitational forces and possible contributions from a hypothetical solar companion or a circumsolar disk. Our findings support earlier work by Oort and others, showing that the observed distribution of comet orbits differs from the equilibrium distribution expected without any loss mechanism—implying that older comets must fade or be removed over time. We test multiple simple fading models and find that the data can be well-represented either by a fading law where the fraction of detectable comets after m passages follows a power law of m⁻⁰.⁶±⁰.¹ (consistent with Whipple’s 1962 proposal) or by a scenario where about 95% of comets survive only a few (~6) perihelion passages, while the rest endure indefinitely. In addition, our analysis provides estimates of key statistics, including the perihelion distribution, the orientation of aphelion directions, encounter frequencies with the giant planets, and the rate at which Halley-type comets are produced.

We present a framework for constructing concordance invariants of knots based on equivariant singular instanton Floer theory equipped with a Chern-Simons filtration. It is shown that various concordance invariants introduced through instanton-based methods in recent work arise naturally within this framework. This connection enables us to compute Kronheimer and Mrowka’s $s^\sharp$-invariant and fractional ideal invariants for two-bridge knots, as well as other related invariants. In particular, we establish a quasi-additivity property of $s^\sharp$, resolving a question posed by Gong. We also define new invariants that share a formal resemblance with Oszváth and Szábo’s Heegaard Floer $\tau$-invariant and Hom’s $\varepsilon$-invariant. Our analysis suggests a precise relationship between these two invariants and the $s^\sharp$-invariant.

Additional topological consequences of our approach include the following. First, we construct a large family of patterns whose induced satellite maps on the concordance group yield images of infinite rank, partially addressing a conjecture by Hedden and Pinzón-Caicedo. Second, we exhibit infinitely many two-bridge knots $K$ that are torsion elements in the algebraic concordance group but for which the set of positive $1/n$-surgeries on $K$ forms a linearly independent subset in the homology cobordism group. Finally, for a knot that is quasi-positive yet not slice, we prove that any concordance from such a knot admits an irreducible $SU(2)$ representation on the fundamental group of its concordance complement.

Although much of the development centers on singular instanton theory under the traceless meridional holonomy condition, we also establish a parallel framework for concordance invariants with arbitrary holonomy parameters, along with some applications in that context.

This study explores wireless sensing in non-line-of-sight (NLoS) scenarios enabled by an intelligent reflecting surface (IRS). An IRS is used to support an access point (AP) in detecting a target located beyond direct line-of-sight. The AP is equipped with multiple antennas, while the IRS features a uniform linear array. The goal is to estimate the target's direction-of-arrival (DoA) relative to the IRS using signals reflected along the AP–IRS–target–IRS–AP path. To enhance estimation accuracy, we jointly optimize the transmit beamforming at the AP and the reflective beamforming at the IRS to minimize the Cramér-Rao lower bound (CRLB) of the estimation error. First, we derive a closed-form expression for the CRLB of the DoA. Then, we minimize this bound through alternating optimization, semi-definite relaxation, and successive convex approximation. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method, which focuses on CRLB reduction, outperforms conventional approaches relying on signal-to-noise ratio maximization and independent beamforming in terms of mean squared error.
