Partition refinement is a technique used to reduce the size of automata and transition systems across various categories. Recently, we introduced a partition refinement algorithm that is broadly applicable—working with different transition types—and achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art methods for specific classes, such as deterministic automata, ordinary, weighted, and probabilistic (labelled) transition systems. The generality of the approach comes from representing transition types using functors on sets and viewing systems as coalgebras. In this work, we extend the runtime analysis to include additional cases, particularly weighted automata and more broadly, weighted tree automata. When weights come from a cancellative monoid, our algorithm matches known optimal bounds; for non-cancellative monoids like the additive monoid of the tropical semiring, it achieves significant improvements in asymptotic performance. Our algorithm is implemented in a flexible, generic tool that can be quickly adapted to specific system types by defining a minimal refinement interface. The framework is modular, enabling easy construction of new partition refiners by combining existing base functors. Experimental results demonstrate that the tool efficiently handles large-scale systems, even those with millions of transitions.

Molecules exhibit chiral excited electronic states that possess an inherent handedness or twist, making them essential in energy-efficient display systems and innovative photosynthetic methods for synthesizing pharmaceuticals. Traditionally, achieving such chiral states involves modifying the twisted geometry of molecular structures—requiring precise control over molecular shape, which introduces significant complexity. In this work, we present a new approach that generates chiral excited states without altering molecular geometry. Instead, chirality arises from the coupling between an achiral molecule and a chiral electromagnetic field, resulting in a hybrid light-matter state known as a chiral polariton. This effect stems from a symmetry-guided, strong interaction between a chiral electromagnetic mode within a nanocavity and an achiral molecule—a phenomenon termed the electromagnetic enantiomer. By leveraging this light-driven mechanism, the need for complex molecular engineering is greatly reduced, offering a simpler pathway to create chiral electronic states. We demonstrate this principle using a system relevant to organic optoelectronic devices, where it enables efficient circularly polarized emission from a non-chiral emitter.

This is the second part of our study focused on classifying the long-term behavior of solutions to a free boundary problem featuring a monostable reaction term in space-time periodic environments. In Part 1 (see \cite{ddl}), we developed a theory guaranteeing the existence and uniqueness of solutions starting from continuous initial data, along with a spreading-vanishing dichotomy. In this work, we adapt methods originally introduced by Weinberger \cite{w1, w2} and extended by others \cite{fyz,lyz,lz1,lz2,lui} to establish the existence of an asymptotic spreading speed under spreading conditions—without requiring beforehand the existence of corresponding semi-wave solutions for the free boundary problem. This approach diverges significantly from earlier studies in the free boundary literature, where the spreading speed was derived after first proving the existence of a semi-wave. In the context of space-time periodic media considered here, obtaining such semi-waves through traditional methods proves to be particularly challenging.

The radial scale length of the exponential disk component of the Milky Way has been measured using near-infrared data. We analyzed the TMGS (Two Micron Galactic Survey) database, which includes positions and K-band magnitudes for approximately 700,000 stars spread across various regions of the galactic plane. To reduce dust extinction and eliminate contributions from the young stellar disc, we focused on regions more than $5^\circ$ away from the plane. We also restricted our analysis to longitudes between $30^\circ$ and $70^\circ$, avoiding contamination from the central bulge, the bar, the molecular ring, and the inner parts of the local arm, as well as from the disc’s warp and truncation in the outer regions. We selected stars with K-magnitudes in the range $9 \leq m_K \leq 10$. The use of the K-band in the near-infrared minimizes extinction effects. Within our selected area, stars of magnitude $m_K = 9.5$ correspond to K2–K5 III-type stars with nearly constant absolute magnitude, greatly simplifying the analysis. Our results yield a radial scale length of $2.1 \pm 0.3$ kpc, a value consistent with typical observations of similar external galaxies.

We have identified a 40 arcsecond-long X-ray filament located at the center of the galaxy cluster A1795. This structure aligns with an Hα+NII filament previously detected by Cowie et al. in the early 1980s and later subdivided into at least two U-band filaments by McNamara et al. in the 1990s. X-ray color ratios indicate that the emission-weighted temperature of the gas along the filament ranges from 2.5 to 3 keV; the actual deprojected temperature will be lower. A comprehensive temperature map of the cluster's core has been produced. The cD galaxy at the filament's leading end appears to be passing through or oscillating within the central region. The radiative cooling timescale for the X-ray-emitting gas in the filament is approximately 3×10⁸ years, consistent with the estimated age derived from the filament’s extent and velocity. This agreement supports the idea that the filament forms via cooling of gas from the intracluster medium. Much of the filament lies distinct from the main body of the cD galaxy and its associated radio source, making it a valuable clue in understanding the energy and ionization mechanisms powering the optical nebular emissions frequently observed in cooling flows.

For an electron-nucleus system, the electron and nuclear pressures are clearly and directly defined using the virial theorem, with the total pressure being their sum. The electron pressure differs from the standard definition—typically given as twice the kinetic energy plus potential energy—because the nuclear virial contribution is subtracted. This distinction is illustrated through multiple definitions of electron pressure discussed in this study. The conventional approach that includes the nuclear virial term in electron pressure is found to be flawed. A similar assessment applies to the definition of the stress tensor in such mixtures. Moreover, it is shown that both electron and nuclear pressures simultaneously vanish in a metal in vacuum, contrary to the common assumption that zero pressure results from the cancellation of non-zero electron and nuclear pressures. Based on these findings, a simple equation of state for liquid metals is developed and numerically evaluated for liquid alkali metals using the quantum hypernetted chain approximation and the Ashcroft model potential.

We carried out ab initio molecular dynamics simulations to investigate the synchronous double proton transfer in the formic acid dimer. To explore how the bonding evolves during this process, we analyzed the electron localization function and molecular orbital isosurfaces. As the two formic acid molecules move closer during the double proton transfer, they form short, strong hydrogen bonds resembling the Speakman-Hadzi type, which are also characterized as polar covalent in nature. Drawing on the concept of resonance-assisted hydrogen bonding introduced by Gilli et al. (J. Am. Chem. Soc., 1994, 116, 909–915), we observe that at the transition state, the proton is linked through a conjugated O–C–O system—clear evidence of a resonance-assisted hydrogen bond. Consequently, the double proton transfer in the formic acid dimer is best described as a resonance-assisted double proton transfer. These results indicate that the synchronous double proton transfer occurs due to two key factors: (i) the formation of Speakman-Hadzi-like short and strong hydrogen bonds, and (ii) the presence of a resonance-assisted hydrogen bond at the transition state.

We investigate the freeze-in mechanism of dark matter within the context of gauge-mediated supersymmetry breaking. In this scenario, a hidden Feebly Interacting Massive Particle (FIMP) couples weakly to the messenger fields while they are still in thermal equilibrium. The FIMP emerges as the fermionic part of pseudo-moduli in a general metastable SUSY-breaking model and is located within the hidden sector. Its relic density and mass are governed by the SUSY breaking scale and the strength of its feeble interaction. In addition to the FIMP, the gravitino—typically the standard dark matter candidate in gauge mediation—also contributes to the overall dark matter abundance through freeze-in. This results in a two-component dark matter content in the hidden sector, with both the FIMP and gravitino originating from the SUSY-breaking hidden sector. We show that the relative abundance of the FIMP compared to the gravitino depends on how SUSY breaking is transmitted to the messenger fields. When the FIMP is the dominant component, the gravitino plays a negligible role. Meanwhile, the neutralino is treated as both the Weakly Interacting Massive Particle (WIMP) in the conventional freeze-out scenario and the Lightest Observable SUSY Particle (LOSP). Within the parameter space of our freeze-in gauge mediation (FIGM) model, we find that the neutralino's contribution to the observed dark matter density is subdominant. Our analysis establishes a direct connection between the SUSY breaking scale in gauge mediation and the FIMP’s freeze-in production rate, offering a natural and predictive framework for understanding hidden-sector dark matter.

Ultraviolet transmission spectroscopy offers a valuable, though underutilized, means of probing the upper atmospheres of exoplanets. To date, UV transit data have revealed the presence of atomic hydrogen, oxygen, and ionized carbon in the upper atmosphere of HD209458b. However, the origin of these spectral features remains debated—there is uncertainty about whether they stem from gas escaping into space and interacting with stellar radiation and the stellar wind, or from the thermosphere confined within the Roche lobe. In this work, we present an empirical model designed to analyze UV transit signals in exoplanets and apply it to interpret HD209458b’s observations in the H Lyα line and the OI triplet emissions. Our analysis shows that the thermosphere maintains an average temperature of 8,000–11,000 K, with the H₂/H dissociation front occurring at pressures ranging from 0.1 to 1 microbar, corresponding to an altitude of 1.1 times the planetary radius (Rp). The thermospheric layer extends up to altitudes of 2.7–3 Rp, beyond which the atmosphere becomes predominantly ionized. We find that the HI transit depth correlates with the optical depth of the thermosphere in the wings of the H Lyα line, while also indicating that the atmosphere exceeds the Roche lobe. Assuming a solar oxygen abundance, we recover an OI transit depth that matches the observed data statistically. An OI transit depth equal to or slightly exceeding that of HI could occur if the planet is experiencing rapid hydrodynamic escape, if the O/H ratio is higher than solar, or if neutral oxygen is present outside the Roche lobe. Given the significant uncertainties in the measurements, further observations are required to more precisely determine OI transit depths and, by extension, refine estimates of the thermospheric composition.

We investigate a decentralized channel assignment challenge in an ad-hoc Internet of Things (IoT) network that operates on spectrum previously licensed to a primary cellular system. Due to limited sensing and probing abilities, as well as constrained computational power, IoT devices are unable to fully obtain detailed Channel State Information (CSI) across multiple shared channels. In real-world scenarios, primary user transmissions exhibit unpredictable behaviors, and dynamic factors such as small-scale fading or device mobility continuously alter channel conditions, introducing stochastic fluctuations in quality. As a result, individual IoT links must adaptively learn channel states in real time using only partial observations and without knowing the characteristics of channels they are not actively using. Moreover, they must achieve a high-efficiency, collision-free channel allocation with minimal coordination. To address this, we model the problem as a contextual multi-player, multi-armed bandit game and introduce a fully decentralized, three-phase policy learning approach based on iterative trial and error. Theoretical results demonstrate that this method ensures all IoT links jointly converge to the socially optimal channel configuration with sub-linear—specifically polylogarithmic—regret over time. Simulation studies show that the proposed approach performs competitively in terms of efficiency and scalability when compared to existing decentralized bandit methods.

This paper introduces a new antenna design—named the movable antenna (MA)—designed to enhance wireless communication performance. Unlike traditional fixed-position antennas (FPAs), which remain stationary, the MA can adjust its location to optimize placement in areas with superior propagation conditions, thereby achieving greater spatial diversity. To represent the typical multi-path environment in a specific area, a field-response-based channel model is formulated under far-field assumptions. In the deterministic scenario, we observe a periodic pattern in the multi-path gain across space, which enables the identification and analysis of the MA's maximum achievable channel gain. For stochastic environments, we derive the expected value of an upper bound on the MA’s peak received gain, depending on the number of independent propagation paths. Our findings indicate that as the number of path components increases, the performance advantage of the MA over the FPA grows significantly—due to stronger small-scale fading characteristics in the spatial domain. Simulation results confirm that the MA system delivers substantial improvements compared to conventional FPAs, and in certain cases, approaches the performance level of a single-input multiple-output (MIMO) beamforming setup.

This study investigates the joint impact of donor (Nd) and acceptor (Li) co-doping at the Zn site in ZnO ceramics on their structural, microstructural, and dielectric characteristics. By integrating experimental findings with first-principles density functional theory (DFT) simulations, we demonstrate that DFT-based modeling can serve as a reliable tool for predicting the dielectric behavior of materials prior to actual synthesis. Various Li- and Nd-co-doped ZnO samples were prepared using the conventional solid-state reaction method. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis confirms the formation of a wurtzite hexagonal phase with space group P63mc. Scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) images reveal randomly oriented, non-uniform grains in both size and morphology. We find that the average grain size and density are key parameters that influence the dielectric response of these materials. While increasing doping levels lead to a reduction in the dielectric constant, the composition Nd₀.₀₀₅Li₀.₀₀₅Zn₀.₉₉O shows a slightly lower dielectric constant (2066 at 1 kHz) compared to pure ZnO, yet it exhibits significantly lower dielectric loss (0.20 at 1 kHz) at room temperature than stoichiometric ZnO sintered at 1623 K. To explore the dielectric relaxation behavior, comprehensive complex impedance analysis was carried out and interpreted in detail. These results offer valuable insights into the development of ceramics with colossal permittivity and deepen the understanding of the underlying CP mechanisms in ceramic systems.

In stochastic circuits, key sources of error include correlation errors, soft errors, and random fluctuation errors—factors that degrade both accuracy and reliability. Soft errors disrupt the correlation state, altering the likelihood of numerical outcomes and ultimately leading to incorrect outputs. This poses a significant risk in safety-critical domains such as security and medical systems, where precise and dependable performance is essential. To address this, we introduce a fault-tolerant method called correlation-sensitive stochastic logic, designed to enhance reliability in stochastic logic elements—specifically AND, XOR, and OR gates. We propose a framework named Remodelling Correlation (ReCo) for stochastic logic elements, enabling stable and accurate operation in combinational circuits. Two distinct ReCo variants are developed, each addressing different design constraints through real-world case studies. The method employs a priority-driven selection process to choose appropriate logic elements and position correction blocks, enabling faster convergence to the desired mean squared error (MSE) with minimal hardware overhead. Crucially, the approach does not compromise the overall circuit’s reliability. The practical efficacy is demonstrated through an image processing task—specifically, contrast stretching of a standard image under noisy conditions. The result shows a structural similarity index of 92.80 for the output image using the proposed method, compared to just 66.43 when errors are present.

Porous acoustic absorbers are particularly effective in the low-frequency range when placed along room edges, making them a widely used solution for minimizing low-frequency reverberation. However, conventional acoustic simulation techniques like ray tracing and mirror source methods fail at low frequencies—due to their reliance on geometric assumptions—leaving a gap in reliable simulation tools for such edge-mounted absorbers. This paper presents a validated finite element model capable of predicting how edge absorbers influence the acoustic field. The model enables detailed analysis of the interaction between the room and the absorber through high-resolution visualizations of the acoustic field within both the room and the absorber material. The finite element model is verified using transfer function data derived from impulse response measurements in a reverberation chamber following ISO 354 procedures. The Basotect absorber is modeled using the Johnson-Champoux-Allard-Lafarge theory, calibrated with impedance tube data obtained via the four-microphone transfer matrix method. Results demonstrate that the model accurately predicts the impact of various edge absorber designs on measured transfer functions. The third-octave band errors, calculated from average spectra, range from 3.25 dB to 4.11 dB.

Several approaches have been developed to enhance a building's seismic performance. Among these, friction dampers serve as effective energy-dissipating devices by absorbing and reducing input energy, thereby minimizing structural displacements. This study explores the feasibility of using the endurance time method to assess the efficiency of friction dampers and to determine their optimal slip load, comparing outcomes from endurance time analysis with those from nonlinear time history analysis. The efficiency indices derived from both methods showed strong agreement. In this investigation, it was assumed that the optimal slip load is the same across all floor levels. The damper strength was then systematically increased in equal increments from zero to one, normalized, to evaluate damping efficiency at each stage. The optimal slip load for the dampers in the steel frame was then determined based on the level achieving the minimum efficiency index. Results show that the endurance time method can effectively replace extensive nonlinear time history analyses, reducing computational effort by 57%. A formula has been developed to estimate the optimal slip load for friction dampers in steel-framed structures based on the structure’s weight. After incorporating the optimized friction dampers and analyzing their impact, it was found that employing the endurance time method—with its superior energy consistency—yields improved results compared to conventional approaches.

Biochemical reactions driven out of equilibrium are thought to maintain stable and reliable performance despite thermal noise and other sources of disorder. Examples of such robust behaviors include sensory adaptation, improved enzymatic specificity, and the persistence of coherent oscillations. These non-equilibrium processes can be described by a master equation in which the rate constants violate detailed balance. We discover that non-equilibrium fluxes can give rise to topologically protected boundary modes, analogous to those observed in electronic and mechanical systems. When a biochemical network can be split into two ordered regions connected by a potentially disordered interface, each region can be characterized by a topological invariant known as a winding number. When these winding numbers differ, the steady-state distribution of molecules is always localized at the interface, regardless of strong variability in reaction rates. Our results suggest a general mechanism by which biochemical systems exploit non-equilibrium dynamics to achieve functional robustness.

Following the theoretical clarification of topological phases in non-interacting electron systems, the link between gapless boundary modes and non-trivial bulk topological features—and how these evolve across dimensions—is now firmly established. Experimentally, however, a clear demonstration of this dimensional hierarchy remains elusive, despite indirect observations such as half-quantized Hall conductance in the quantum Hall effect and extrapolated results from quantum oscillation measurements. In this study, we observe the emergence of a chiral edge mode in the surface state of three-dimensional topological insulators under the influence of magnetic fields, providing experimental support for the dimensional hierarchy. By applying laser pulses to the surface state of Bi₁₋ₓSbₓ, we find that the voltage relaxation sign in one edge flips relative to the other exclusively when a magnetic field is applied to the topological insulator phase. This sign reversal is attributed to the chirality of the edge states, as confirmed through coupled time-dependent Poisson and Boltzmann equations.

Channel state feedback is crucial for enhancing link performance in modern wireless systems, becoming even more vital in future networks. However, this feedback consumes valuable uplink resources, resulting in increased overhead. This paper examines the effects of channel state feedback and presents an optimized approach to minimize this overhead in real-world systems. By adopting a more precise wireless channel model, we derive theoretical lower bounds for both periodic and aperiodic feedback mechanisms. These findings serve as a basis for optimizing feedback system design, including the bit allocation for channel state quantization. Based on this analysis, we propose a practical feedback method that reduces overhead and outperforms existing solutions like zero-holding. Simulation results validate its effectiveness and highlight its promise for deployment in next-generation wireless networks.

This paper presents a mathematical examination of singular (nonsmooth) artifacts introduced in X-ray CT reconstructions via filtered backprojection (FBP) methods when data is arbitrarily incomplete. We demonstrate that such artifacts originate at the boundary of the measured data. Depending on the geometric characteristics of this boundary, two distinct artifact categories emerge: object-dependent and object-independent artifacts. Object-dependent artifacts stem from discontinuities or singularities within the scanned object and often manifest as line-like extensions; these generalize the well-known streaking effects seen in limited-angle tomography. In contrast, object-independent artifacts are largely independent of the object’s internal structure and appear in one of two forms: straight-line streaks when the data boundary is nonsmooth at a point, or curved patterns when the boundary is locally smooth. We establish that these streaks and curves are the only possible singular artifacts that arise in continuous FBP reconstructions. The study also includes detailed characterizations of the artifact intensity within Sobolev function spaces in specific scenarios. These findings are relevant to classical incomplete-data problems such as limited-angle and region-of-interest tomography, as well as emerging practical imaging configurations. Numerical examples—based on both simulated and real datasets—are presented to validate the theoretical results, including a case study involving synchrotron data where line-like artifacts appear without any connection to the object structure.

Several published models for the Stanford Question Answering Dataset (Rajpurkar et al., 2016) demonstrate poor robustness, showing more than a 50% drop in F1 scores when evaluated using the AddSent (Jia and Liang, 2017) adversarial method. Retraining these models on data created by AddSent fails to substantially improve their resilience. To address this, we introduce AddSentDiverse, a new adversary generation technique that enhances diversity in adversarial training data by producing high-impact examples that penalize models for relying on superficial assumptions. Additionally, to strengthen resistance against AddSent’s semantic perturbations—such as antonym substitutions—we simultaneously enhance the model’s ability to understand and learn semantic relationships. By combining these improvements with our adversarial data augmentation strategy, we achieve a significant boost in robustness: a 36.5% increase in F1 score across various adversarial evaluations, without compromising performance on the standard SQuAD task.

In 2006, Prochter and colleagues found a statistically significant increase in the occurrence of very strong Mg II absorption systems along lines of sight to gamma-ray bursts (GRBs), compared to those seen in quasar lines of sight. This counterintuitive finding has prompted numerous astrophysical interpretations—such as dust obscuration or gravitational lensing—but none have clearly resolved the anomaly. Leveraging the most comprehensive dataset of GRB afterglow spectra to date, we reassess the claim of enhanced absorption. In an independent sample of GRB spectra covering a survey path three times larger than that of Prochter et al., we determine the fractional incidence per unit redshift of Mg II absorbers with rest-frame equivalent widths of at least 1 Å at redshifts around 1 to be l(z) = 0.18 ± 0.06. This value aligns well with known rates observed in quasar sightlines. Consequently, we find no evidence supporting the original enhancement and conclude the earlier result likely stemmed from a statistical fluke. While some traces of the original effect persist in our full dataset—namely, a roughly 1.5-fold excess of Mg II absorbers in GRBs over quasar lines—its statistical significance now stands at approximately 90% confidence level. When limiting our analysis to high-resolution spectra of GRB afterglows, which overlap significantly with Prochter et al.’s data, we still detect a significant excess of Mg II absorption. However, the origin of this excess, if genuine, remains uncertain, as there appears to be no apparent link between the rapid follow-up observations using echelle or echellette spectrographs and the detectability of strong Mg II doublets. A larger collection of high-resolution GRB spectra is needed to better understand this observed discrepancy.

Social networks serve as primary platforms where individuals share opinions on new products, emerging social trends, and political developments through interpersonal interactions. Identifying the origin of such information—whether it's a rumor's first spreader or a trendsetter—is often critical. However, this task is inherently difficult; in standard tree-like diffusion structures, detecting the source from a single snapshot has a theoretical upper limit of just 31% accuracy. In real-world scenarios, source identification typically requires active querying, such as asking “Are you the origin of the rumor?” or “Who initially told you about it?”—which significantly increases detection chances. This paper examines two query types: (a) batch queries and (b) interactive queries with directional information, under the realistic assumption that responses may be false with a certain probability. We develop estimation methods for both query types and analytically evaluate their detection efficiency, as well as the additional resource cost due to untruthful answers. Our results demonstrate that querying substantially enhances detection accuracy. Extensive simulations on both synthetic and real social network structures confirm these analytical findings.

We evaluate the large-scale structure and evolution of galaxy clusters using a suite of TREESPH simulations, enabling a statistically robust comparison with existing X-ray observations. The analysis relies on "power ratios," a methodology first introduced by Buote & Tsai. We consider three cosmological models: CDM, LCDM (with Ω_L = 0.7), and CHDM (including one dark matter neutron and Ω_h = 0.2). All models are scaled to achieve a comparable cluster number density. For each model, we carry out a P3M simulation in a large simulation volume, identifying the 40 most massive clusters. We then trace these back to the initial redshift and perform hydrodynamical TREESPH simulations for each cluster. This approach allows a statistical assessment of cluster morphology under each cosmological model, compared to ROSAT data, using Student’s t-test, F-test, and Kolmogorov-Smirnov (K-S) test. The K-S test—and its extension to two-dimensional distributions—are also applied to compare the joint distributions of two or three power ratios. Our results show that using dark matter instead of baryonic matter—how some studies do—leads to systematic biases, since baryons tend to form a more disorganized structure than dark matter. We find that the three models exhibit distinct behaviors in these tests: LCDM performs the worst, while CDM and our CHDM models yield similar results. Although these models capture the general trends in power ratio distributions, further improvements are likely achievable through modifications in dark matter composition or by adopting a non-flat CDM scenario.

We report optical emission-line spectra from extended HII regions located in the neutral gas disk surrounding the blue compact dwarf galaxy NGC 2915. By combining strong-line R23 diagnostics with direct oxygen abundance measurements, we observe a flat to slightly increasing metallicity gradient extending out to 1.2 times the Holmberg radius. The outer portion of the disk reaches a metallicity of 0.4 times the solar value. Analysis of metal yields reveals that the outer disk is overabundant relative to its gas fraction, whereas the central star-forming region is underabundant in metals compared to its own gas content. Star formation rates, derived from deep ~14 ks GALEX FUV observations, suggest that the weak star formation at large radii cannot account for the observed oxygen abundances at those galactocentric distances. We propose three physically plausible explanations for the metal-enriched outer gaseous disk: radial transport of metals from the central regions, powerful galactic outflows followed by fallback, and infall from external galaxy accretion. These findings have important implications for understanding the origins of the mass-metallicity relation in gas-rich dwarf galaxies.

We introduce a gradient-based optimization method for designing stellarator coils by shaping the coil-winding surface. Building on the REGCOIL approach (Landreman 2017, Nucl. Fusion 57, 046003), which determines coil geometries using a continuous current potential, we employ the adjoint method to compute accurate derivatives of the objective function. This enables efficient derivation of analytical gradients, avoiding the inaccuracies and noise typically associated with numerical approximations. By including the root-mean-squared current density as a key target in the objective, we enhance the engineering performance of the coil design. For the W7-X and HSX configurations, our optimized winding surfaces reduce the normal magnetic field at the plasma boundary while simultaneously increasing the average distance between the coils and the plasma compared to the original surfaces. The resulting coils exhibit reduced toroidal span, lower curvature, and greater inter-coil spacing. Additionally, we develop a visualization tool to explore how changes in the winding surface’s normal displacement affect key performance metrics, offering insights useful for assessing engineering tolerances.

Electron collision dynamics with carbon monoxide (CO) have been investigated using R-matrix methods, with a focus on detecting possible higher-lying resonances beyond the well-known low-lying $^2\Pi$ state near 1.6 eV. Comprehensive assessments of basis sets, target potential models, and scattering approaches are conducted. The final analysis employs the larger cc-pVTZ basis set in a 50-state close-coupling calculation. Resonances are identified and characterized using both the Breit-Wigner eigenphase sum and time-delay techniques. These methods reveal a very narrow $^2\Sigma^+$ Feshbach-type resonance situated just above the excitation threshold of the b $^3\Sigma^+$ state, which is predicted at 12.9 eV. This resonance appears in the cc-pVTZ-based close-coupling calculation but is not observed in the cc-pVDZ-based one. In the asymptotic region, the electronic structure of CO$^-$ is examined, revealing 45 molecular states that dissociate into a negative ion and a neutral atom. These states are analyzed in the limit of large internuclear distances to understand their behavior. The study also provides quantitative data on total, elastic, and electronic excitation cross sections. The implications of these findings for explaining dissociative electron attachment in CO observed around 10 eV are discussed.

Building on the analysis of the four secular resonances between nearby orbits presented in Li and Christou (Celest Mech Dyn Astron 125:133–160, 2016) (Paper I), we extend the semianalytical model to cover both prograde and retrograde orbits, establishing a one-to-one correspondence between resonances in the two cases. We adopt a generalized form for the critical angle, expressed as a linear combination of the apsidal and nodal differences between the two orbits: $ b_1 \Delta \varpi + b_2 \Delta \Omega $. This yields a family of secular resonances, among which the ones previously investigated in Paper I are particularly strong. Testing the model in regions close to massive satellites—such as Jupiter’s Himalia and Saturn’s Phoebe—with physical and orbital characteristics similar to those of these irregular moons reveals that more than 20% and over 40% of the phase space, respectively, is influenced by these resonances. The long-term stability of the resonances is validated through numerical integration of the full Newtonian equations of motion. Results indicate that the lowest-order resonances, with $ b_1 + |b_2| \le 3 $, persist, and even higher-order resonances, up to $ b_1 + |b_2| \ge 7 $, remain viable. Depending on the satellite mass, between 10% and 60% of the test particles undergo capture into these secular resonances, consistent with the phase space predictions from the semianalytical framework.

We present a novel metric to measure the amount of local information nodes must acquire to collaboratively solve a graph-related problem. We establish that the local information cost (LIC) serves as a natural lower bound on the communication requirements of distributed algorithms. In the synchronous CONGEST-KT1 model—where each node starts with knowledge of its neighbors’ identities—we prove that any $\tau$-round algorithm that makes an error with probability at most $\gamma$ must communicate at least $\Omega(\textsf{LIC}_\gamma(P) / \log \tau \log n)$ bits to solve a graph problem $P$. This result is the first to provide a general trade-off between communication and time in the CONGEST-KT1 model.

We illustrate the application of LIC by deriving a communication lower bound for constructing a $(2t-1)$-spanner with at most $O(n^{1+1/t + \epsilon})$ edges, where $\epsilon = \Theta(1/t^2)$. Our main finding is that any distributed algorithm running in $O(\textsf{poly}(n))$ time must transmit at least $\tilde\Omega((1/t^2) n^{1+1/2t})$ bits in the CONGEST model under the KT1 assumption. Prior work only established a trivial $\tilde\Omega(n)$ communication lower bound for this spanner construction.

A key implication is that it is impossible to simultaneously achieve optimal running time and optimal communication in a distributed spanner algorithm. Building on the results of King, Kutten, and Thorup (PODC 2015), this indicates that minimum spanning tree construction can be substantially faster than spanner computation when communication is constrained to $\tilde O(n)$ bits. Furthermore, our result also provides time complexity lower bounds for spanner construction in the node-congested clique model (Augustine et al., 2019) and in the push-pull gossip model with limited bandwidth.

We study the quenched fraction of central and satellite galaxies as a function of their stellar mass, host halo mass, and the large-scale matter density of their environment. The matter density is derived from the ELUCID simulation, a locally constrained model based on SDSS data, while halo masses and galaxy classifications are drawn from the group catalog of Yang et al. Across all three environmental factors, the overall quenched fraction increases consistently. We find that the environmental quenching efficiency—defined as the quenched fraction depending on halo mass—is independent of stellar mass. This independence explains the previously observed stellar mass insensitivity in density-dependent quenching. When analyzing centrals and satellites separately, both populations exhibit similar relationships between quenching efficiency and halo or stellar mass, indicating comparable quenching histories within their host halos. Our analysis shows that satellite quenching alone is insufficient to explain the full environmental quenching efficiency of the combined galaxy population. The observed differences between centrals and satellites primarily stem from the fact that galaxies of the same stellar mass typically inhabit halos of different masses. After accounting for these halo and stellar mass effects, a weak but detectable residual dependence on environmental density remains—this dependence disappears when halo assembly bias is considered. These findings suggest that halo mass is the dominant environmental factor controlling quenching in both central and satellite galaxies.

The transition of a spheroidal particle from a paraelectric to a ferroelectric state, along with its dynamic susceptibility, is analyzed in the paraphase without relying on approximations. It is assumed that surface charge is fully compensated, leading to a boundary condition where the polarization vanishes at the particle's surface—specifically, P = 0. Within the quasistatic framework, the dynamic dielectric function exhibits an infinite set of resonance frequencies. The dielectric response characteristics in the paraphase are examined in detail. The transition temperature decreases as the particle diameter diminishes, following an inverse quadratic dependence. A critical diameter is identified: below this threshold, the ferroelectric phase ceases to exist. The theoretical predictions are compared with experimental data. Incorporating a finite dead layer thickness greatly improves the theoretical fit to experimental results for PbTiO₃, and yields satisfactory agreement for BaTiO₃ as well.

Graph-structured data are prevalent across numerous applications, and a core challenge lies in measuring graph similarities for tasks like classification. R-convolution graph kernels offer a positive-semidefinite approach by breaking down graphs into substructures and comparing them. However, a key limitation in practical implementation is that these substructures are interdependent, resulting in a high-dimensional feature space. Additionally, traditional graph kernels struggle to model complex, higher-order relationships among vertices. To address both issues, we introduce DeepMap—a framework designed to learn deep representations for graph feature mappings. The output for each graph is a compact, low-dimensional vector that effectively encodes intricate high-order interactions within a vertex’s neighborhood. DeepMap generalizes Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) to arbitrary graphs by creating aligned vertex sequences and constructing a receptive field for every vertex. Experiments on multiple graph classification datasets show that DeepMap outperforms existing methods, achieving state-of-the-art results.

Let $\ccM_{g,[n]}$, defined when $2g-2+n>0$, denote the stack of stable algebraic curves of genus $g$ with $n$ unordered marked points.  
Looijenga introduced Prym level structures to build smooth, projective Galois coverings of the stack $\ccM_g$.  
In Section 2, we define Looijenga level structures, a generalization of Looijenga’s original idea, which yields a tower of Galois coverings of $\ccM_{g,[n]}$ equivalent to the full tower of geometric level structures over $\ccM_{g,[n]}$.  
Section 3 provides a geometric interpretation of these level structures in terms of moduli spaces of curves possessing symmetry. This leads to a straightforward criterion for their smoothness. Under mild conditions, the criterion confirms that Looijenga level structures are smooth.  
The second part of the paper, beginning in Section 4, focuses on describing the D-M boundary of level structures. Section 6 gives a detailed account of the nerve of the D-M boundary of abelian level structures. Section 7 demonstrates how this construction can be used to approximate the nerve of Looijenga level structures. These findings are then applied to develop a novel approach to the congruence subgroup problem for the Teichmüller modular group.

Probabilistic load forecasting (PLF) plays a crucial role in the advanced tool suite needed for effectively managing smart energy grids. Neural networks are frequently favored for their ability to capture intricate, nonlinear relationships between forecasted loads and input features, offering flexible and accurate predictions. Yet, deriving reliable and detailed uncertainty estimates from these complex, opaque models remains a significant challenge. In this study, we introduce a new PLF method based on Bayesian Mixture Density Networks. The approach simultaneously models both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainties, enabling the estimation of general conditional probability distributions that adapt to input variables through an end-to-end training process. To ensure accurate and efficient inference of posterior distributions, we combine Mean Field variational inference with deep ensembles. Results from household-level short-term load forecasting tasks demonstrate the method’s effectiveness and robustness across varying operational scenarios.

Compression fractures of the vertebral bodies serve as early and dependable indicators of osteoporosis. While these fractures are detectable on CT scans, they are often overlooked by radiologists during routine clinical assessments. Although previous studies have validated the effectiveness of automated vertebral fracture classification methods, many current approaches produce results that are difficult to interpret and can fail in complex cases—such as those involving severe pathological changes or scoliosis. We introduce a two-phase algorithm that first localizes the vertebral column in 3D CT data and then identifies individual vertebrae and quantifies fractures in 2D. Using a straightforward 6-point annotation protocol aligned with current clinical standards, we train neural networks for both stages. Our method does not require any exclusion criteria, runs in just two seconds on a single GPU, and delivers clear, easily understandable outputs. It achieves performance comparable to that of expert radiologists, setting new benchmarks in 3D vertebral localization (average error: 1 mm), 2D vertebral detection (precision: 0.99, recall: 1), and fracture diagnosis (ROC AUC at the patient level: 0.93).

Glaucoma is a serious eye condition that can result in vision loss if left untreated. Traditionally, ophthalmologists diagnose it by reviewing multiple medical images from various imaging devices. This process is both time-consuming and costly. In this work, we introduce a new computational method for detecting and locating glaucoma using only fundus images, processed through cutting-edge deep learning models. Our solution combines Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for diagnosis and Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping (Grad-CAM) for identifying the affected regions. Results from a small dataset—without segmentation labels—show promising performance, achieving a diagnostic accuracy of 0.91±0.02 and an ROC-AUC of 0.94. Additionally, we have developed a publicly accessible web tool that hosts our predictive model, aiming to make reliable glaucoma screening available to broader audiences.

The precise observation of orbital period shortening in compact-star binaries with periods around 0.1 days is well accounted for by gravitational wave (GW) radiation from inspiraling systems in vacuum. Yet, an often-overlooked influence—the dark matter dynamical friction (DMDF)—arises from the interaction of binary components with the gravitational wakes generated by dark matter. This DMDF depends on both the orbital period and the local dark matter density, meaning its strength varies with the binary’s position within the Galaxy. We compute the DMDF for three dark matter distributions: the Navarro-Frenk-White (NFW), the non-singular isothermal sphere (NSIS), and the Ruffini-Argüelles-Rueda (RAR) model based on self-gravitating keV fermions. We first confirm that for binaries involving neutron stars (NS) and white dwarfs (WD)—including NS–NS, NS–WD, and WD–WD systems—gravitational wave emission dominates over DMDF, given the existing observational constraints. Then, we determine under what conditions DMDF becomes comparable to or even exceeds GW effects. For example, for NS–WD systems of mass 1.3–0.2 $M_\odot$, NS–NS pairs of 1.3–1.3 $M_\odot$, and WD–WD binaries of 0.25–0.50 $M_\odot$, located at 0.1 kpc, the DMDF begins to compete with GW-driven decay at orbital periods of 20–30 days in the NFW model, whereas in the RAR model this crossover happens around 100 days. When binaries are nearer to the Galactic center, DMDF grows stronger, causing the critical orbital periods to decrease significantly. We further explore parameter regimes where DMDF results in orbital expansion rather than contraction. Together, these findings suggest that detecting or constraining the DMDF effect in compact binaries could provide strong insights into the properties of dark matter and its spatial distribution in our Galaxy.

Real-world networks dynamically change through the addition or deletion of nodes and connections. In existing models of network evolution, node degrees often fluctuate or increase without bound. A recently proposed family of models—degree-preserving network growth (DPG)—maintains constant node degrees, producing network structures that are both distinct and more varied than those generated by earlier approaches ([Nature Physics 2021, DOI: 10.1038/s41567-021-01417-7]). Notably, even with this degree constraint, the DPG framework successfully reproduces the output of several established network growth models. Simulations demonstrate that many empirically observed real-world networks can emerge from small initial graph configurations.

In this work, we initiate a rigorous mathematical investigation of the DPG model family. We establish that the degree sequences of several well-known network growth models can be exactly reconstructed through the DPG process, provided appropriate parameter settings are used. Furthermore, we prove that the general question of whether a given simple graph can be generated by a DPG process from a small initial seed—referred to as DPG feasibility—is computationally intractable, being NP-complete. An open and significant question remains: Is there an inherent structural feature that explains why many real-world networks are indeed constructible via the DPG mechanism?

In earlier research, we derived the explicit form of nonlinear partial differential equations—along with the corresponding boundary conditions—that govern the transport coefficients in systems lying outside Onsager's region. These equations are derived without omitting any term from the fundamental balance laws (mass, momentum, and energy), which makes them a strong candidate for modeling transport phenomena in turbulent thermodynamic systems. In a specific scenario involving two thermodynamic forces, the resulting nonlinear PDE takes a form in thermodynamic field theory (TFT) analogous to Liouville's equation in Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian geometry. The reliability of our model is demonstrated through a case study involving transport in Tokamak plasmas—a situation where Onsager's relations fail to align with experimental data. We perform a detailed analysis of electron mass and energy losses in turbulent plasmas from the FTU (Frascati Tokamak Upgrade) facility, showing a close match between theoretical results and experimental measurements. This methodology enables the analytical determination of the Bohm and gyro-Bohm coefficients, representing a first-time analytic evaluation of these parameters. The objective of this research line is to extend our approach to the Divertor Tokamak Test Facility (DTT), currently under construction in Italy, and to the International Thermonuclear Experimental Reactor (ITER).

Photocatalytic hydrogen evolution on untreated TiO₂ suffers from poor performance, primarily because of rapid charge carrier recombination and slow electron transfer kinetics. To enhance efficiency, noble metal nanoparticles—such as Pt, Pd, or Au—are commonly used as cocatalysts. However, their high cost has spurred interest in cheaper alternatives. In this work, we present a method to create a highly effective, noble metal-free photocatalytic system for hydrogen production using alloyed NiCu cocatalyst nanoparticles deposited on anodic TiO₂ nanotube arrays. These NiCu bilayers are deposited via plasma sputtering and then subjected to thermal treatment, which induces dewetting. During this process, nickel and copper diffuse across the surface and spontaneously form NiCu nanoparticles at the nanotube walls. This process enables precise tuning of critical nanoparticle properties, including composition, work function, and hydrogen-evolution activity. NiCu nanoparticles with equal Ni and Cu ratios demonstrate significantly higher catalytic activity compared to either pure Ni or Cu nanoparticles and achieve hydrogen evolution rates competitive with those observed in systems using conventional Pt-based cocatalysts.

This paper examines charged electroweak interactions in de Sitter spacetime. We construct a reduction scheme for the Proca field using solutions of interacting field equations, and employ perturbation theory to define transition amplitudes to first order. The formalism is applied to analyze the spontaneous emission of fermions, antifermions, and $W^{\pm}$ bosons in an expanding de Sitter universe. Our findings extend the Weinberg-Salam electroweak model to curved spacetime, specifically in the context of $W^{\pm}$ boson interactions with leptons. The transition amplitudes and corresponding probabilities are found to depend on the Hubble parameter. Both analytical and numerical results demonstrate that these perturbative processes occur only under conditions of large early-Universe expansion. For such large-scale expansion, we compute the total transition probability and rate, evaluating the momentum integrals via dimensional and Pauli-Villars regularization. Finally, we recover the Minkowski limit, in which the emission probabilities vanish, confirming the established result that spontaneous production of $W^{\pm}$ bosons and fermions is prohibited in flat spacetime due to conservation of energy and momentum.

Motivation: The biomedical literature is rich in chemical-protein interactions (CPIs), making their automatic identification crucial for advancing drug development, personalized medicine, and fundamental research. While current methods primarily rely on sentence-level sequences to detect CPIs, additional valuable insights can be derived from the local sentence structure and external biomedical knowledge. Incorporating these elements could significantly enhance CPI extraction accuracy. Results: We introduce a novel neural network framework designed to boost CPI detection. The model begins by using BERT to generate high-quality, context-aware embeddings for the title, instance, and knowledge sequences. To model local sentence structure, we integrate a Gaussian probability distribution, capturing patterns within the instance sequence. Additionally, an attention mechanism is employed to selectively combine title-level and external knowledge features. These fused representations are then combined and passed through a softmax function to predict CPIs. Our method is evaluated on the CHEMPROT dataset, where it outperforms existing state-of-the-art approaches. Experimental findings indicate that the Gaussian distribution and external knowledge complement each other, jointly improving extraction performance. Moreover, the Gaussian distribution proves particularly effective in handling sentences with overlapping or ambiguous relations—common in biomedical context. Availability: The dataset and code are available at https://github.com/CongSun-dlut/CPI_extraction. Contact: yangzh@dlut.edu.cn, wangleibihami@gmail.com Supplementary information: Supplementary materials are accessible via Bioinformatics online.

Deep learning has emerged as a powerful approach in software development, delivering exceptional results on a wide range of complex problems. While deep neural networks (DNNs) are now widely adopted, their high computational demands make them unsuitable for resource-limited environments. Quantization has gained traction as a viable solution, enabling significant reductions in model size without sacrificing much accuracy—preserving performance while drastically lowering resource requirements. The resulting quantized neural networks (QNNs) offer enhanced energy efficiency. However, despite their benefits, quality assurance methods like testing and formal verification remain underdeveloped for QNNs, even though they are just as critical as for traditional floating-point networks. In this paper, we introduce a novel and efficient formal verification method specifically tailored for QNNs. Our key contribution is a new encoding that transforms the QNN verification problem into a set of integer linear constraints—problems that can be efficiently solved using existing commercial solvers. This encoding is both sound and complete. We apply our technique to verify local robustness and compute the maximum robustness radius. A prototype tool, QVIP, has been developed to realize our approach, and we perform comprehensive evaluations. Experimental results on QNNs across varying quantization levels show that our method is not only significantly faster—achieving up to two orders of magnitude improvement— but also capable of solving more verification tasks within the same time budget compared to current state-of-the-art techniques.

We analyze the meromorphic operator-valued function $1 - K(z) = 1 - \frac{A(z)}{z}$, where $A(z)$ is holomorphic in a domain $D$ and takes values in the class of compact operators on a fixed Hilbert space. Assuming that $A(0)$ is selfadjoint and possibly of infinite rank, we examine the behavior of the characteristic values of $1 - K(z)$—that is, the complex numbers $w$ for which $1 - K(w)$ fails to be invertible—near the origin. We prove that, generically, these characteristic values approach zero at the same rate as the eigenvalues of $A(0)$.

These abstract findings are then applied to the study of resonances associated with the operator $H = H_0 + V$, where $H_0$ is the 3D Schrödinger operator with a constant magnetic field of scalar strength $b > 0$, and $V$ is a real electric potential with an appropriate decay at infinity. It is known that the spectrum of $H_0$ is purely absolutely continuous, covering the interval $[0, \infty)$, and that the Landau levels $2bq$, where $q$ ranges over the integers, act as thresholds in this spectrum. We investigate the asymptotic distribution of resonances close to any specific Landau level and, under generic conditions, derive the leading asymptotic term of the resonance counting function, expressed explicitly in terms of suitable Toeplitz operators.

As technology continues to evolve, mobile learning (m-Learning) presents substantial potential, particularly in higher education. Despite growing interest and a number of pilot projects carried out in universities, there remains a gap in understanding how institutions can effectively leverage m-Learning to enhance teaching and learning processes. While many research efforts have been published, a unified framework of key success factors influencing the adoption of m-Learning in academic settings is still missing—largely because these studies have not been systematically reviewed or synthesized. This study employs meta-analysis to conduct a comprehensive evaluation of existing research on m-Learning, identifying critical success factors. The findings highlight that, from students’ perspectives, the most valued benefits of m-Learning include collaborative learning experiences, the flexibility of learning anytime and anywhere, and intuitive, easy-to-use application designs.

Many soft and biological materials exhibit "soft glassy" behavior, characterized by anomalous, random motion and occasional cooperative rearrangements of their components. A similar pattern emerges in stock price movements, though the underlying causes remain unclear. Recent simulations of a foam have shown that this behavior arises from the system exploring a high-dimensional configuration space through gradual energy minimization across a slowly shifting, fractal energy surface. We demonstrate that the key geometric properties of such landscapes can be identified and measured both in simulations and in real-world, high-dimensional datasets. In a mayonnaise-like dense emulsion, experimental observations of oil droplet motion reveal that the fractal structure of both the trajectory paths and the energy landscape drive the anomalous diffusion and collective reorganization—closely mirroring simulation predictions. Applying this same method to individual stock prices within the S&P 500 index yields strikingly comparable outcomes, suggesting that stock price dynamics stem from movements across a similar, slowly evolving, high-dimensional fractal energy field.

Let $\phi: M \to M$ be a smooth, compact, connected diffeomorphism of a manifold, and let $X$ be its mapping torus. There is a natural fibration $p: X \to S^1$, and let $\xi \in H^1(X, \mathbb{Z})$ be the associated cohomology class. For a nonzero integer $\lambda \in \mathbb{Z}^*$, define $\phi_k^*$ to be the induced endomorphism on the degree-$k$ cohomology of $M$, and let $J_k(\lambda)$ denote the largest size of a Jordan block corresponding to the eigenvalue $\lambda$. Consider the representation $\rho_\lambda: \pi_1(X) \to \mathbb{C}^*$ given by $\rho_\lambda(g) = \lambda^{p_*(g)}$, and let $H^*(X, \rho_\lambda)$ be the twisted cohomology of $X$ with respect to $\rho_\lambda$. We show that $J_k(\lambda)$ equals the maximal length of a nonzero Massey product of the form $\langle \xi, \ldots, \xi, a \rangle$, where $a \in H^k(X, \rho_\lambda)$ and the length is the number of $\xi$ terms. In particular, if $X$ is strongly formal—such as a Kähler manifold—then all Jordan blocks in $\phi_k^*$ are of size one. When $X$ is formal, the Jordan blocks corresponding to eigenvalue 1 are also size one. This allows us to construct simple examples of mapping tori that are formal but not strongly formal. The proof relies on the identification of certain Massey products with differentials in a Massey spectral sequence, which can be explicitly determined from the Jordan normal form of $\phi^*$.

This study examines the stability of laser sail-inspired spacecraft built from dielectric metasurfaces with areal densities below 1 gram per square meter. We demonstrate that by carefully engineering the microscopic optical forces acting on such metasurfaces from a high-power laser (100 GW), passive self-stabilization is possible—resulting in the sail being optically trapped within the main laser beam and capable of autonomously correcting both angular and lateral deviations. The metasurfaces in question comprise a carefully arranged array of beam-steering units that reflect light at varying angles and intensities. Each unit is tailored in design across the surface, and we employ optical force modeling to analyze how different scattering characteristics affect interaction with laser beams of distinct intensity patterns. Using full-wave numerical simulations, we compute the actual optical forces on silicon/silicon dioxide metasurfaces composed of over 400 elements, and compare these results to our theoretical predictions. Our findings indicate that specific metasurface configurations, under first-order approximations, can enable stable, controlled propulsion of laser sail-type spacecraft.

Consider a directed graph $G$ with $n$ vertices and a designated root vertex $s$. The directed minimum degree spanning tree problem involves finding an incoming spanning tree rooted at $s$ that minimizes the maximum in-degree among all vertices in the tree. This problem is NP-hard, as it contains the Hamiltonian path problem as a special case. The best known polynomial-time solution based on linear programming provides an approximation ratio of $\Delta^* + 2$, where $\Delta^*$ is the optimal value of the maximum tree in-degree. For purely combinatorial algorithms—those that do not rely on linear programming—the best known approximation is $O(\Delta^* + \log n)$, established by Krishnan and Raghavachari in 2001, though this comes with quasi-polynomial time complexity. In this work, we focus on developing purely combinatorial algorithms to close the performance gap between linear programming methods and combinatorial ones. We present a new purely combinatorial algorithm with polynomial time complexity that achieves an approximation of $O(\Delta^* + \log n)$. Furthermore, we enhance this method to yield a $(1+\epsilon)\Delta^* + O\left(\frac{\log n}{\log \log n}\right)$ approximation for any constant $0 < \epsilon < 1$, while maintaining polynomial running time.

Animals can quickly recognize, from minimal experience, that different state-action combinations yield the same rewards and transition patterns. In contrast, current reinforcement learning systems must learn through extensive trial and error to determine when various state-action pairs are value-equivalent—a process that often demands far more environmental interactions than is practical. To address this, MDP homomorphisms have been introduced to compress the observed MDP into a simpler, abstract version, enabling more efficient policy learning. When such a homomorphism can be pre-defined—typically by leveraging known symmetries in the environment—significant gains in sample efficiency have been observed. We introduce a new method for building homomorphisms in settings with discrete actions. This approach uses a partial model of the environment to identify which state-action pairs lead to identical next states, effectively shrinking the state-action space by a factor equal to the number of available actions. We refer to this as equivalent effect abstraction. In a gridworld experiment, we show that this method enhances sample efficiency in model-free learning and accelerates planning in model-based approaches. Additionally, in the CartPole task, our method achieves better performance than existing homomorphism learning techniques while using 33 times less training data.

How do selection, mutation, and horizontal gene transfer collectively influence the distribution of observable traits in bacterial or cellular populations?  

Although horizontal gene transfer—where genetic material is exchanged between individuals—plays a critical role in helping organisms adapt, its effect on the density of phenotypes within a population remains inadequately understood.  

We analyze an elliptic integro-differential equation that models the evolutionary steady state of phenotypic density in an asexual population. Under conditions of low mutational variability, we identify the equilibrium solutions arising from the balance among resource competition, mutation, and horizontal gene transfer. Our results indicate that, for specific parameter ranges, polymorphic equilibria can emerge—meaning phenotypic density can cluster around multiple distinct traits. These multi-peak equilibria arise from a counteracting dynamic between selection and horizontal gene transfer. In contrast, models that ignore gene transfer typically result in equilibria with a single dominant phenotype.

This paper presents a finite element approach for solving partial differential equations on hypersurfaces embedded in $\mathbb{R}^N$, where $N = 2$ or $3$. Instead of discretizing directly on the surface, the method relies on restricting bulk finite element functions to the surface, using their traces. The bulk mesh is constructed on an octree grid that adapts dynamically—either refining or coarsening—based on error indicators and estimates of surface curvature. The Cartesian structure of the bulk grid enables a straightforward and efficient adaptation process, eliminating the need to conform the mesh to the surface geometry. As a result, the total number of degrees of freedom aligns naturally with the two-dimensional character of surface PDEs. No explicit surface parametrization is required; the surface can be defined implicitly via a level set function. In practice, a modified version of the marching cubes algorithm is applied to reconstruct the surface with second-order accuracy. The work establishes optimal convergence rates in both $H^1$ and $L^2$ surface norms for smooth solutions and quasi-uniform mesh refinement. Numerical tests on problems with less regular solutions also confirm optimal convergence in terms of degrees of freedom, provided the grid adaptation is guided by suitable error indicators. Extensive numerical experiments are conducted across diverse surface geometries and problem types, including advection-diffusion equations. The theoretical analysis and computational results together demonstrate that combining Cartesian adaptive meshes with unfitted (trace) finite elements yields a practical, effective, and robust method for solving PDEs on surfaces.

Travel times of compressional (P) and shear (S) waves play a vital role in earthquake and exploration seismology. Accurate and efficient computation of these travel times is fundamental to the effectiveness of such techniques. Solving the Eikonal equation—especially in anisotropic media with complex phase velocity fields—however, remains a significant technical challenge. The Eikonal equation is a first-order, hyperbolic, nonlinear partial differential equation that models the high-frequency limit of the wave equation. While fast marching and sweeping methods are widely used for their computational efficiency, they often produce inaccurate results in settings involving sharp variations, irregular terrain, and complex wave velocity structures. This paper introduces a novel approach to solving the Eikonal equation using the peridynamic differential operator (PDDO). By incorporating an internal length parameter (called the horizon) and a directional weight function, the PDDO reformulates the equation in a nonlocal framework. This method inherently avoids issues arising from abrupt changes in field or model parameters and consistently tracks the direction of wave propagation. The weight function governs how strongly neighboring points within the horizon influence each other, enabling stable solution construction without relying on upwind schemes. The proposed method is validated through several examples involving different Eikonal formulations applied to complicated anisotropic velocity models. The results exhibit unconditional numerical stability and show excellent agreement with benchmark solutions.

The behavior of quantized vorticity within neutron stars underpins most theories explaining pulsar glitches. Yet, the sheer number of vortices—on the order of $ \approx 10^{13} $—and the vast difference in scale between the femtometer-sized vortex cores and the kilometer-sized stellar body render direct quantum dynamical simulations utterly impractical. In this work, we present an initial effort to incorporate vortex dynamics into large-scale hydrodynamic models of superfluid neutron stars via a mean-field approach. Focusing on a one-dimensional configuration, we demonstrate that vortex clustering and differential rotation in the superfluid result in propagating wave-like structures, or "avalanches," as solutions to the superfluid velocity equations. We introduce a new variable—representing the fraction of free vortices—and explore various ways to model its transport with the superfluid flow. Our results show that these added terms produce solutions with a linear growth phase during a glitch event. In certain configurations, they can also generate glitch precursors and, in some cases, lead to frequency drops, known as anti-glitches.

The CALICE collaboration is developing high-performance electromagnetic and hadronic calorimeters for use in future International Linear Collider detectors. For the electromagnetic calorimeter, the current design features a high-granularity sampling approach using tungsten as the absorber material and silicon as the active sensing component. A physics prototype comprising thirty active layers has been built. Each layer covers an area of 18×18 cm² with 1×1 cm² pads. The total absorber thickness reaches 24 radiation lengths. This prototype underwent beam tests at DESY and CERN in 2006 and 2007, exposing it to electron and hadron beams across a broad spectrum of energies and incident angles. This paper outlines the structure of the prototype and its data acquisition system, provides a summary of the beam test data collected in 2006, and details the methods employed for pedestal subtraction and calibration. The signal-to-noise ratio measured is 7.63 ± 0.01. Certain electronic effects were identified, including coherent noise and crosstalk between adjacent pads, as well as signal leakage between active and passive regions. The results on detector uniformity and long-term stability are also discussed.

The original "Counterfactual" definition of causal effects was developed to ensure unbiasedness and accuracy—not to support generalizability. In this work, we introduce a straightforward definition of External Validity (EV) for interventions and counterfactual scenarios. This definition enables the construction of EV metrics for individual counterfactuals and non-parametric effect estimators for groups of counterfactuals—essentially for sampled datasets. By applying this framework, we address longstanding challenges in the standard counterfactual approach: out-of-sample validity, dependence on independence assumptions or estimation procedures, simultaneous estimation of multiple effects and full models, bias-variance trade-offs, statistical power, omitted variables, and links to modern predictive and explanatory methods.

Methodologically, our definition transforms the original parametric and often ill-posed estimation problems into combinatorial enumeration tasks suitable for non-experimental data. Using this approach, we extend widely used supervised, explanatory, and causal effect estimators, enhancing their performance across three key dimensions—External Validity, Unconfoundness, and Accuracy—and enabling their use in non-independent, non-identically distributed (non-i.i.d.) samples. We show improvements in out-of-sample prediction, intervention effect forecasting, and causal effect estimation. The COVID-19 pandemic underscored the critical need for learning models capable of making reliable predictions from limited, often incomplete data. We illustrate the practical utility of our framework in addressing this urgent real-world challenge.

With the growing everyday use of advanced cameras, individuals in contemporary society are increasingly focused on obtaining high-quality photos. Yet, image quality often falls short of expectations because of noise interference. Consequently, effectively removing noise while maintaining key image characteristics has become a critical need. Existing denoising techniques rely on specific assumptions about the probability distribution from which the noise is drawn to achieve optimal results. In this work, we apply a recently developed Gramian-based filtering approach to eliminate noise originating from five common probability distributions, using selected images. Unlike traditional methods that operate on individual pixels, our technique divides the image into patches and preserves smoothness across the image. By performing denoising on the underlying manifold of the patch space rather than directly in the image space, it retains important visual features. We evaluate its performance using three standard benchmark images and compare it against two leading denoising algorithms: BM3D and K-SVD.

The Kepler mission identified a unique group of exoplanets called "super-puffs"—planets with masses only a few times that of Earth but radii exceeding Neptune's, resulting in extremely low average densities. All three known planets in the Kepler 51 system, a young solar-type star, fall into this category. This system offers a valuable chance to compare the internal structures and atmospheric properties of these enigmatic worlds, potentially revealing insights into their origin and development. Using the Wide Field Camera 3 (WFC3) on the Hubble Space Telescope, we observed two transits each for Kepler 51b and 51d. By combining these new transit observations with reprocessed Kepler data and refined stellar characteristics, we confirmed that all three planets have densities below 0.1 g/cm³. Our analysis of the WFC3 transmission spectra between 1.15 and 1.63 μm showed no detectable features, indicating no absorption signals greater than 0.6 scale heights (assuming hydrogen-helium-dominated atmospheres)—thus, ruling out significant water vapor presence. The flat transmission spectra are best explained by the presence of a high-altitude aerosol layer on each planet, with atmospheric pressures below 3 mbar. When combined with previously observed featureless spectra in other sub-Neptune planets, this finding supports one of the two models proposed by Crossfield and Kreidberg (2017): planets with cooler equilibrium temperatures tend to host more aerosols at high altitudes. Conversely, we strongly reject the alternative hypothesis suggesting that the hydrogen-helium mass fraction causes pronounced transmission features.

This paper focuses on nonparametric estimation in instrumental variable (IV) regression settings. Over recent years, several flexible machine learning approaches have been introduced for IV estimation, yet they often face at least one of the following drawbacks: (1) requiring the IV model to have a unique solution; (2) providing error bounds in terms of pseudometrics—such as projected norms—rather than valid norms like the $L_2$ norm; or (3) relying on a strong assumption known as the closedness condition, which demands sufficient smoothness of a conditional expectation operator. We introduce the first estimator and corresponding theoretical analysis that overcomes all three limitations while allowing general function approximation. Our approach features a novel penalized minimax estimator that converges to a well-defined IV solution even in cases where multiple solutions exist. We establish a strong $L_2$ convergence rate under relatively mild assumptions—specifically, only requiring standard source and realizability conditions, without invoking the closedness condition. We note that the source condition and the closedness condition are fundamentally incompatible, and eliminating the closedness requirement represents a significant advancement over existing methods that depend on both. This improvement is enabled by reinterpreting the IV estimation problem through a new framework of constrained optimization.

Privacy-preserving machine learning techniques commonly rely on differential privacy (DP) to protect sensitive data during model training. In practice, methods achieve DP by introducing randomness—such as adding Gaussian noise to clipped gradients—when updating model parameters. We show that this randomness leads to predictive multiplicity: for the same input, differently randomized training runs can yield substantially different predictions, even when using the same dataset. This variability in predictions—known as predictive multiplicity—has not been examined in depth, nor is it currently assessed or disclosed to developers or decision-makers. We establish a theoretical bound on how many retrainings are needed to reliably estimate this multiplicity. Through both theoretical analysis and comprehensive experiments, we evaluate predictive multiplicity across three DP-preserving approaches: output perturbation, objective perturbation, and DP-SGD. Our findings reveal that as privacy guarantees grow stronger, predictive multiplicity increases, and this variation is not uniformly distributed across individuals or demographic groups. Since the training randomness directly influences predictions for certain examples, our results expose a significant concern for the reliability and accountability of decisions made by differentially private models in critical applications. We therefore recommend that practitioners perform predictive multiplicity audits before deploying such models in contexts where individual outcomes are at stake.

The influence of Fe and Ni catalysts on the production of carbon nanotubes (CNTs) via atmospheric pressure chemical vapor deposition (APCVD) was examined. FESEM observations indicate that nanotube growth occurs via a tip-based mechanism. HRTEM results reveal that Ni catalyzes the formation of multiwalled CNTs exhibiting a bamboo-like morphology, whereas the Fe catalyst produces straight, fully filled tubes. XRD data confirm the graphitic structure of the resulting nanotubes, with no detectable carbide phases in either case.
