At that point, the residence time of particles within a cell becomes dependent on the local density of the opposing species. Our analysis of particle positional fluctuations reveals non-standard diffusion patterns, exhibiting long-tailed and asymmetric distributions that align well with known chromatographic distributions. Furthermore, we find that the presence of the opposing crowd enhances the spatial spread of the primary particles compared to the non-biased scenario ($\alpha = 0$), although this increase is accompanied by a slight reduction in dispersion.

In quantum state discrimination, observers are presented with a quantum system and tasked with identifying its state among two or more predefined possibilities. In the context of local quantum state marking—an extension of this problem—there are N composite quantum systems and N mutually orthogonal target states. Two spatially separated parties, Alice and Bob, must accurately identify and mark the states of these systems using only local operations and classical communication. In this work, we examine local state marking for N = 4, 5, 6, and 7 systems, each of dimension 4 ⊗ 4. Alice and Bob are allowed to perform three types of local operations: measuring either the σz or σx observable locally, and implementing entanglement swapping. We find that for any set of four such 4 ⊗ 4 systems, Alice and Bob can successfully achieve perfect local state marking. For N = 5 and N = 6, perfect marking is possible only for specific target states. We also propose that local state marking is impossible when seven target states are involved, as even the most straightforward case cannot be fulfilled with the allowed operations.

When heavy ions such as carbon are used in therapeutic treatments, the particle beam undergoes nuclear fragmentation, generating secondary light charged particles—especially protons and alpha particles. To accurately assess the radiation dose delivered to tumors and nearby healthy tissues, precise predictions of these secondary particles’ fluences are essential. Currently, only a few double-differential carbon fragmentation cross sections are available within the energy range employed in hadron therapy (40 to 400 MeV/u). To address this gap, new experimental measurements have been carried out to determine the double-differential cross sections of carbon interacting with various thin targets. This report presents the experimental findings from a study conducted at GANIL in May 2011. The double-differential cross sections and angular distributions of secondary fragments resulting from 12C fragmentation at 95 MeV/u on thin targets—carbon, CH₂, aluminum, Al₂O₃, titanium, and PMMA—have been measured. The experimental configuration is detailed, the sources of systematic uncertainties are discussed, and all collected data are presented.

We report infrared and Raman studies of magnetite (Fe₃O₄), a material that exhibits both a metal-insulator transition and a structural phase change—the Verwey transition—at T_V = 120 K. Below this temperature, the optical conductivity shows a pronounced gap-like reduction below 1000 cm⁻¹. The structural nature of the Verwey transition is evident in the emergence of new infrared- and Raman-active phonon modes. While the frequencies of the infrared-active phonons remain unchanged across the transition, their spectral linewidths broaden significantly. In contrast, the Raman-active phonon at 670 cm⁻¹ undergoes a sharp shift in both frequency and linewidth at T_V. For temperatures below T_V, fine features appear in both infrared and Raman spectra, suggesting strong anharmonicity in the lattice. We estimate the effective electron mass above T_V to be approximately 100 times the free electron mass (m). Our results support a polaronic model for electrical conductivity and highlight the crucial role of electron-phonon coupling in driving the Verwey transition.

A group of excited atoms can emit light in phase and cooperatively—a phenomenon called superradiance—when the ensemble's size is smaller than the wavelength of the emitted photons. This collective emission is highly sensitive to the surrounding electromagnetic (photon) environment. The dense population of microwave photons from 300 K blackbody radiation (BBR) greatly accelerates the decay of Rydberg states to nearby energy levels, enabling superradiance that cannot occur through spontaneous decay in pure vacuum. In this work, we observe superradiance in ultracold Rydberg cesium atoms immersed in a medium of room-temperature photons. We directly track the time-dependent decay of Rydberg $|nD\rangle$ states to $|(n+1)P\rangle$ states in free space. Simulations support the observed enhancement of superradiance due to blackbody radiation in large atomic ensembles. We show that van der Waals forces between Rydberg atoms alter the dynamics of superradiance and shift its scaling behavior. When static electric fields are applied, the superradiant emission slows down, likely due to dephasing induced by many-body interactions. Our findings reveal key aspects of many-body quantum dynamics in interacting atoms coupled to thermal blackbody photons, and may pave the way for developing microwave-frequency blackbody thermometry through collective, dissipative interactions between photons and atoms.

The Pacific Ocean Neutrino Experiment (P-ONE) is a proposed neutrino telescope designed for a cubic-kilometer volume, intended for deployment in the deep sea of the northeastern Pacific Ocean. In partnership with Ocean Networks Canada’s optical deep-sea data and communication infrastructure, an international team of researchers plans to install a network of kilometer-scale mooring lines at a depth of approximately 2660 meters in the relatively flat Cascadia Basin—located about 300 miles west of Vancouver Island. As the project enters its design and development phase, the P-ONE collaboration seeks input from oceanographers and marine scientists to contribute expertise and experience in deploying additional or complementary instrumentation and measurement approaches for oceanographic investigations. Beyond monitoring bioluminescence and deep-sea thermal and dynamic processes, the array could also incorporate active and passive acoustic systems. This communication outlines the P-ONE detector design and highlights a range of potential research areas relevant to oceanography and marine science.

We present a method to ensure high resource availability in a networked, heterogeneous multi-robot system despite individual resource failures. In our framework, resources such as sensing and computation are distributed across the robots, which collaborate on joint tasks by pooling these capabilities. When a resource on a specific robot fails—such as a sensor malfunctioning—the system reconfigures by enabling that robot to access the resource through cooperative communication with other units. Specifically, we address the problem of selecting which communication links to retain in the network after a failure occurs. To evaluate the performance of the resulting resource distribution, we introduce a metric that quantifies the quality of resource allocation within the network topology. Upon a resource failure, the system dynamically reorganizes the network to restore the distribution as closely as possible to an ideal configuration, while minimizing the increase in communication overhead. Our approach leverages integer semi-definite programming to achieve this balance. Additionally, we incorporate a simulated annealing algorithm to determine a robot formation that respects the required inter-robot distances dictated by the network structure and other constraints. The entire process—communication topology design, spatial configuration, and motion planning for formation transitions—is executed in just a few seconds. We evaluate the effectiveness of our method through both simulation and real-world tests using a team of seven quadrotor drones.

We present a technique for discretizing planar domains that are C2-regular and embedded within non-matching triangular meshes. The approach involves defining mappings from background triangles to curved, conforming triangles that precisely align with the immersed boundary. These mappings are built using a new parameterization strategy that projects the boundary onto a set of nearby edges via closest-point pr
